title.rendered: Practical techniques for inclusive meeting management

content.rendered:
Being in a meeting where the same people are always talking and the truth comes out after the meeting is over significantly reduces the quality of the organization's decision making. Inclusive meeting management is a technology that brings out the insights of all participants. In this article, we will introduce 10 practical techniques.

Pre-meeting preparations

1. Pre-sharing your agenda

Share agendas and talking points more than 24 hours in advance. Even members who are not good at improvisation can prepare and participate.

2. Request for pre-loading related materials

Instead of taking the time to read the material at the beginning of the meeting, the premise is that you will read it to yourself in advance. The meeting is positioned as a "place to read and discuss materials".

3. Selection of Participants

The principle of participating “because I can contribute” rather than “because I am called”. Spectators are not invited, and participants are responsible for actively contributing.

Facilitation during the meeting

4. Say one word to everyone at the beginning

During the first two to three minutes of the meeting, everyone has time to talk. Taking the first step of your speech as a group will increase your participation rate later on.

5. Observe and intervene in speech bias

The facilitator observes the amount of speech consciously. If a particular person keeps talking, if a particular person has never spoken, explicitly shake it to someone else.

6. "What else do you think?" frequently "

When you hear an opinion, say, “How do other people feel?” to intentionally elicit additional input. The effect of preventing sympathetic pressure on the first opinion.

7. Using text chat

For those who are not good at speaking up, we will ask for their opinions in the chat. Especially useful for remote meetings. Facilitator reads the chat out loud and shares it with everyone.

8. Make use of 5 Seconds of Silence

After the question, wait 5 seconds even if there is no immediate response. Allowing time to think can lead to deeper answers.

Follow up after the meeting

9. Documenting and publishing actions

Document and share meeting decisions, homework, and next steps. Prevents “say/don't say”.

10. Follow up individually after the meeting

Members who spoke little during the meeting were later asked individually, "Do you have any additional comments?" ”, indicating that the meeting is not the only opportunity to express an opinion.

4 actions to avoid

1. Get in the middle of what you're saying

Blocking is a message that your opinion doesn't matter. The discipline of listening to the end.

2. Immediately reject the idea

Instantly denying “it's hard” or “I've done it in the past” reduces psychological safety. Say something like, “Interesting idea, tell me more about it.”

3. Use a lot of inner ring tape

Abbreviations and contexts known only to veteran members exclude new members and external participants. Consciously add an explanation.

4. Acceptance of the position that monopolizes the discussion

Letting a position holder monopolize the discussion reduces other members' willingness to contribute. The facilitator is responsible for intervening.

Effect from a long-term perspective

Organizations that continue to have inclusive meetings will be better able to make better decisions and capitalize on the diversity of their organization. Increasing the number of people who feel they deserve to be in the meeting also makes the meeting itself more efficient.

excerpt.rendered: Meetings in which only loud speakers speak do not make use of diverse perspectives. From a facilitator's perspective, we will explain 10 practical techniques to enable “inclusive meetings” where everyone can contribute with peace of mind.

title.rendered: インクルーシブな会議運営の実践テクニック
Document Culture in Remote Development Organizations

content.rendered:
The productivity of a remote development organization depends heavily on the quality of the document culture. In-person verbal information sharing must be documented remotely. In this article, we will organize specific measures to root the "writing culture" and failure patterns.

Why document culture is decisive

Reason 1: The foundation of asynchronous collaboration

Documented information is essential for members working in different time zones to work together. Organizations that rely on verbal tacit knowledge are reliably clogged with remoteness.

Reason 2: Accelerated onboarding

Documentation is the primary way new members understand your organization. Quality documents can take up to three months to catch up in one month.

Reason 3: Verifiability of decision

Because the reasons for past decisions remain in the form of documents, we can later validate why we decided to do so. Organizational learning builds.

5 ways to foster a culture of writing

Option 1: Document First Principles

Based on the principle of "creating a document before the meeting". A meeting is a “place to read and discuss documents” and abolish meetings that discuss from scratch.

Measure 2: Minutes are mandatory and company-wide

Require meeting minutes for all meetings and make them viewable company-wide. Default disclosure policy, except for certain confidential information.

Policy 3: Introduction of Architecture Decision Records (ADRs)

Important technical decisions are documented as ADRs. Leave "Why did you make this choice?" "What were the other options?"

Policy 4: "What is not in the document is written by the newcomer" rule

When a new member asks a question to elicit tacit knowledge, the new member documents the answer. This ensures that the documentation is continuously enriched.

Measure 5: Evaluate document quality

Include “Team Document Quality” in the manager's endorsement. Without it, you won't have time to write.

3 traps to avoid

Trap 1: Perfectionism

If you think you should only publish documents with a high level of completeness, you will not be able to write anything. Even in the WIP (Work in Progress) state, it is necessary to have a culture of publishing.

Trap 2: Leaving old documents behind

If there are any old documents left, the new members will be confused. Set aside time for document inventory once a quarter.

Trap 3: Tool Rampage

Mixing Notion, Confluence, Wiki, Google Docs, Markdown files, etc. will dissipate information. The "original is here" is clearly determined.

Concept of tool selection

Emphasis on long-term storage and searchability

Knowledge bases such as Notion, Confluence, etc. Accumulated as an organization's knowledge asset.

Discussion/Collaboration Focus

Real-time collaboration tools like Google Docs, Figma, and Miro. As a place for generation and editing.

Code Adjacency Information

GitHub READMEs, Wikis, and Discussions. Information to place close to the code.

The Road to Fixation

Document culture does not take root in three to six months; it requires a year to two years of continuous awareness and leadership leadership paradigm (management writes a lot). Once rooted, it serves as a long-term competitive advantage for the organization.

title.rendered: リモート開発組織のドキュメント文化
3 Career Questions to Handle 1: 1

content.rendered:
When one-on-one agendas are focused on day-to-day work, long-term career dialogue opportunities are lost. It is too late and too infrequent to talk about your career with just one evaluation interview per year. This article explains the 3 career questions that you should incorporate into 1: 1s and how to use them effectively.

3 questions

Question 1: “What would you like to do in three years?”

Questions that elicit a long-term perspective. Instead of asking for a specific job title, ask “What kind of problems do you want to work on?” and “What kind of impact do you want to have?”

Question 2: “So what do you want to learn in the next six months?”

Questions that connect long-term goals to medium-term skill development. Unleash specific skills and experiences rather than vague aspirations.

Question 3: “As an organization, what can I do to support it?”

Questions that demonstrate an organization's commitment to supporting personal growth. Provide tangible support, such as training opportunities, new roles, and mentoring settings.

How to use each question

How to Listen to Question 1

Begin with a shallow answer (“I want to be a manager” or “I want to deepen my expertise”), and after 3-4 repetitions, you will see an intrinsic preference. Answering the question itself can also encourage your introspection.

How to Listen to Question 2

Together, we will think about what we can do in six months to get closer to three years. It is important for managers to materialize in their own language rather than giving answers. When asked, “I want to gain management experience,” I asked, “What experience specifically?" I will dig deeper.

How to Listen to Question 3

Instead of your manager saying, “I can do this,” pull the request from you. Get specific requests, such as “I want to be trained,” “I want to be involved in a new project,” or “I want to talk to someone from another department.”

Frequency and Operations

Approximately once a quarter

If you deal with 3 questions in each 1: 1, it becomes too heavy. Once a quarter, it is realistic to set aside time as "career 1: 1".

Manager Preparation

Before your career 1: 1, take notes to reflect on your preferences from past conversations. "I said 100 last time, is there any change?” with continuity.

Keep Records for

Answers to the three questions will be recorded and changes will be observed on a semi-annual/yearly basis. The change in orientation reflects the person's growth and environmental impact and is worth understanding as an organization.

THINGS THEY AVOID

Impose your manager's preferences

Induction, such as “I think you deserve a managerial position,” distorts the person's true orientation. A manager's job is to bring out their own aspirations and help them achieve them.

Confused with evaluation interview

Career 1: 1s are not a place to evaluate. By discussing "current evaluation" and "future direction" separately, a place where the person can speak openly is established.

Effect from a long-term perspective

Organizations that have regular career conversations increase retention and intrinsic motivation. A sense that your organization is taking its future seriously is a motivating factor that goes beyond pay. One of the strongest players in the prevention of turnover.

excerpt.rendered: 1: 1s that are biased towards everyday business. Includes an implementation guide that explains the “three questions” for embedding long-term career dialogue, as well as the purpose of each question and how to listen effectively.

title.rendered: 1on1 で扱うべき「キャリアの3問」
Visualize the psychological debt of the organization

content.rendered:
“Technical debt” is now widely recognized, but the organization also accumulates “psychological debt.” This includes unresolved interpersonal issues, unspoken grievances, and memories of unjust decisions. If left unvisually, it will gradually undermine the performance of the organization. In this article, we will organize concepts and visualization methods.

What is Psychological Debt?

Psychological debt is the total amount of “unprocessed emotions and memories” accumulated within an organization. If technical debt is "moving but not beautiful code", psychological debt is "moving but not healthy relationships and memories".

The Three Sources of Psychological Debt

Source 1: Unresolved interpersonal issues

"Conflict at that time", "A word said", "Evaluation that felt unfair", etc. are not handled while remaining in the minds of the parties. On the surface, they work together, but there is a big shadow on the build-up of trust.

Source 2: Unsurfaced Dissatisfaction

A state in which a dissatisfaction that has been given up "does not change even if I say it" is latent in the organization. Although it does not appear in the questionnaire, it can be observed in a way that the air always cools down in certain topics.

Source 3: Past decisions that felt unfair

Memories of past layoffs, ratings, promotions, etc. Apart from the validity of the actual judgment, the perception that it was unfair also affects the trust in the current decision.

4 ways to visualize

Method 1: Anonymous “Tissue Health Check” Survey

Conduct a quarterly quantitative survey. “Do you have any unresolved issues?" "How much do you trust the organization?" "" How frank? "” and other indicators of psychological debt.

Method 2: Facilitated retrospective

A place to bring in external facilitators and discuss “what we can't handle as an organization” with the whole team. About once every six months. Since it is difficult to be honest when the manager is the host, an external perspective is required.

Method 3: Retirement interview aggregation analysis

Aggregating the feedback of the Leavers reveals a pattern of “issues that couldn't be handled” by the organization. A genuine treasure trove that is difficult for incumbents to come by.

Method 4: Candid information sharing between managers

A team of managers meets once a month to share “hotspots” for each team. Across the organization, you may see patterns of repetition.

3 Ways to Pay Off Your Debt

Approach 1: Provide a transparent space for dialogue

Provide a space for the organization to discuss unresolved issues head-on. An attitude such as "Let's explain and talk again about that decision in the past" will lead to the elimination of debt.

Approach 2: Improvements don't repeat the same issue

If past problems are caused by a system or mechanism, review the system. An organization's commitment not to repeat the same issues rebuilds trust.

Approach 3: Candid acknowledgement from management

The message “I'm aware of these issues in the past” and “I'm sorry” symbolize the organization's candor. It is important to face the front without hiding it.

Effect from a long-term perspective

Organizations that regularly visualize and repay psychological debt can maintain strong cohesion and frankness over the long term. Conversely, even if it is working on the surface, the vitality of the tissue will be greatly reduced in 3 to 5 years. Just like technical debt, it is an area that requires regular investment.

excerpt.rendered: Similar to technical debt, there is also a buildup of “psychological debt” in the organization. We will explain the concept of psychological debt that corrupts the organization when left unvisually and the specific methods to visualize it both quantitatively and qualitatively.

title.rendered: 組織の心理的負債を可視化する
Prevent Project Retrospective from becoming corrupted

content.rendered:
Retrospectives are widely implemented in Scrum-implementing companies. However, in many organizations, retro has become a ritual of "writing a routine KPT and ending it". In this article, we will organize the design that prevents disintegration and produces essential learning.

3 symptoms of disfigurement

Symptom 1: Same problem every time

"Long meetings" and "few documents" are written in the Problem column every time, but nothing changes. This is a sign that Try has no implementation capability or has not reached the root cause of the problem.

Symptom 2: Try is abstract

No abstract try like “communicate more” or “be more efficient” is performed. Unspecific actions do not change anything as a result.

Symptom 3: Everyone shut up

"There is no particular problem" "It is going well" becomes the majority. It is necessary to determine whether there is really no problem or whether the psychological safety is low and cannot be said.

5 ways to prevent bodily disfigurement

Idea 1: Change the format every time

Rotate multiple retro formats such as KPT, Mad/Sad/Glad, Start/Stop/Continue, Sailboat, 4Ls, etc. In the same format, the brain goes into "regular" mode, which reduces deep awareness.

Idea 2: Refine the theme

“Let's look back at it all,” they argue. If you narrow down the themes of "this time only communication" and "this time only quality", you can dig deeper.

Devise 3: 5 Whys thoroughly

When you find a problem, be sure to ask “Why?” ”5 times. Dig until you reach the root cause, not a superficial symptomatic treatment.

Idea 4: Try is smart

Try meets the smart criteria of Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant and Time-bound. Specify up to the level of "by next week, X will document X".

Idea 5: Validate last try first

At the beginning of each try, check the progress of the previous try. Validate if it was executed or effective and then create a discipline to move on to the new Try.

Facilitator’s Role:

Static

The structure is fixed when the manager is always the facilitator. Various progression styles are created when members take turns as facilitators.

Don't be afraid of silence

When the discussion stops, allow 5-10 seconds of silence instead of prompting immediately. Time to think creates deep awareness.

Immediate intervention in personal attacks

Immediately intervene in a personal attack such as "because" and reframe it as a problem of how it works.

Retro itself retro

Once a quarter, we will carry out "Retro Retro". A place to reflect on and improve the retro process itself. Without it, the retro will become obsolete.

Effect from a long-term perspective

Teams with functional retrospectives will continue to improve their learning speed and anchor a culture of organizational improvement. “Reflective organizations” gain a competitive advantage in the long run.

excerpt.rendered: Does the retrospective at the end of the sprint end with the usual KPT? With practical techniques, we will explain retro design that prevents debris and produces essential learning.

title.rendered: プロジェクトレトロスペクティブの形骸化を防ぐ
The essence of Nordic work-life integration

content.rendered:
"Work-life balance" is an idea to "balance" work and life as a separate thing, but in the Nordic countries, the idea of "work-life integration" is the mainstream. The two are not separate, but designed to enrich each other. This article organizes the essence and implementation.

Balance vs. Integration

The Idea of Balance

The idea of viewing work time versus life time as a competitive relationship and allocating the two in an "appropriate ratio". The premise is that if you work more, your life will decrease.

Integration Ideas

The idea of designing work and life as two interacting elements rather than as separate variables. Assume a complementary relationship where quality of work enhances quality of life and quality of life supports creativity at work.

The Four Elements of Nordic Integration

Element 1: Time Flexibility

Opening and closing times can fluctuate according to the rhythm of an individual's life. The flexibility to incorporate child transportation, hospital visits, hobby practice, etc. during business hours is institutionalized.

Element 2: Flexibility of location

You can choose according to your business characteristics and personal situation, such as completely remote, hybrid, and work center. The premise is that fixing the location is not essential to the outcome.

Component 3: Workload flexibility

Workload can be adjusted according to the life stage, such as working shorter hours during childcare, sabbatical leave (a few months of leave), and choosing a full working period.

Element 4: Evaluation flexibility

Designed to function fairly even when workload fluctuates. By using achievements and contributions as the axis of evaluation rather than time, the above three types of flexibility work.

Practical application to Japanese companies

Phase 1: From time flexibility

Starting with the abolition of core time and the introduction of the flex-time system. The introduction hurdle is the lowest, and the effect is felt quickly.

Phase 2: Location Flexibility

How many days of the week can you be remote, and document your business-specific policies. Designed to minimize mandatory days at work.

Phase 3: Volume Flexibility

Consideration of a time-short work system and a sabbatical system corresponding to childcare, nursing care, and re-learning. This requires not only system design, but also linkage with the evaluation system.

Phase 4: Evaluation flexibility

Eliminate time-based metrics and move to outcome-based. At the same time, clarify the definition of "results" (short-term results vs. long-term contributions).

Timeline to fixation

These changes will take three to five years to establish themselves as a culture, even if they are institutionalized. Continuous communication from management and leadership paradigms (management itself practicing flexible work styles) determine the speed at which it will take hold.

Benefits of integrations

Short-term effects

Increased employee engagement and talent acquisition.

Medium-term effects

Decrease in turnover and increase in the proportion of female managers.

Long-term effects

Improve the creativity of the organization, strengthen the ability to create innovation, and improve social evaluation.

Notes

Confusion arises when “flexibility” is misinterpreted as “everything is free.” Flexibility is “freedom based on clear rules and mutual trust.” Freedom without rules is irresponsible.

excerpt.rendered: A Nordic way of working that goes beyond "work-life balance" to achieve "work-life integration". The essence and implementation method in the Japanese context will be explained from both the cultural background and the organizational system.

title.rendered: 北欧型 work-life integration の本質
Team Building 12 Experiments in the Remote Era

content.rendered:
When you hear “team building remotely”, many people imagine a “cross-screen drinking party”, but this is not effective. There are many team building techniques that have been developed with remote assumptions. In this article, we present 12 validated experiments by effect.

Category 1: Experiments to deepen relationships

1. Lifetime Timeline Sharing

Each member shares the important events of their life in chronological order. About 30 minutes. The understanding of each other's backgrounds is deepened at once.

2. Virtual walk 1: 1

One-on-one time, voice calls while both parties take a walk. Screen-free conversations lead to relaxed conversations.

3. Hobby presentation session

Once a month, members present their hobbies in 10 minutes. Sharing aspects outside of work increases your sense of three-dimensionality as a person.

Category 2: Experiments to create a shared experience

4. Online Escape Game

Team-based escape game with paid services. The natural division of roles occurs in cooperative play, and the strengths that are usually invisible can be seen.

5. Remote Reading Session

Read the same books and discuss once a month. Thinking habits and values are shared. Recommended books related to organizational issues.

6. Simultaneous cooking session

Each person makes the same recipe online at the same time. Shared experiences, including failures, are memorable.

Category 3: Work-Related Learning

7. Pairing (not limited to programming)

Time for two people to work on a task at the same time. It can be applied regardless of job type, such as sales, design, and analysis. Knowledge transfer accelerates.

8. Failure Sharing

Once a month, a place where each person shares what they have recently done. The soil of psychological safety is nurtured.

9. Demo Day

Once a quarter, each member demonstrates their deliverables in 5 minutes. Visualize the work of other members who are not usually visible.

Category 4: Outside Business Connections

10. Shuffle lunch

Online lunch for 2-3 people with a random match once a week. Can be automated with Donut Bots, etc.

11. Family Day

A casual gathering that invites family members (partners and children) to an online meeting. I can see the background of the home worker.

12. Virtual training camps

2-3 days of intensive work + chat sessions once a year. It serves as an alternative to physical training camps.

Three principles of experimental design

1. Don't force participation

Once participation is mandatory, fun becomes “work.” We will continue to participate voluntarily.

2. Do it during business hours

Outside of working hours, attendance drops. If you see value as a company, incorporate it during business hours.

3. Do not force effect measurement

If you ask for 'measure ROI', your activity will shrink and you will observe it as a change in the air in your organization from a long-term perspective of three to six months.

How to proceed with the implementation

You don't have to try all 12. First, choose 2-3 and try for 3 months to see the effect and compatibility and decide to continue/replace. There must be something that fits the culture of the organization.

excerpt.rendered: Migrating in-person team building to a remote location will not work. Organize 12 team building experiments designed with remote assumptions and their effects and precautions.

title.rendered: リモート時代のチームビルディング 12の実験
Structured interviews that remove cognitive bias from hiring interviews

content.rendered:
Hiring interviews are where the interviewer's perception bias is most pronounced. Unconscious biases such as "first impression", "homogeneity bias", and "halo effect" distort hiring decisions. This article explains how to minimize bias in the design of structured interviews.

The Three Problems of Unstructured Interviewing

Question 1: Homogeneity bias

Tends to rate “people like me” highly. The diversity of the organization is impaired and the breadth of thinking is narrowed in the long run.

Question 2: Halo effect

A phenomenon in which one strong characteristic (educational background, past company affiliation, first impression) also affects other evaluation items. The accuracy of the overall judgment is greatly reduced.

Question 3: Variability between interviewers

Even for the same candidate, the evaluation varies greatly depending on the interviewer. The phenomenon of "A passes and B fails" is frequent.

The Five Elements of a Structured Interview

Element 1: Pre-defined evaluation axis

Break down “Ability required to succeed in this position” into 5-7 items in advance. Document the evaluation criteria (5 levels) for each item.

Element 2: standardizing questions

Ask all candidates the same questions in the same order. Improvised questions are minimized. This will allow comparisons between candidates.

Element 3: Gathering Behavioral

"What would you do? Instead of a virtual question like,“ What have you actually done in the past in a situation like this? "star method (Situation, Task, Action, Result).

Element 4: Immediate Independent Assessment

Immediately after the interview, before the interviewers discuss with each other, each person independently fills out the evaluation. Leave a rating before being influenced by the opinions of others.

Element 5: Calibration at Evaluation Meeting

After the independent assessment, all interviewers will work together to complete the assessment. For each evaluation axis, if there is any variation, discuss the reasons and form a consensus.

Operational Points

Interviewer Training

Structured interviews don't just “know how” to work. Maintain and level the interviewer's skills with biannual training and quadrennial calibration meetings.

Include diverse perspectives

Allow the interviewer team to have diversity, such as gender, age, job type, and years of tenure. This avoids biased judgments from a single point of view.

Post-hire tracking

The evaluation at the time of the interview and the performance after joining the company will be tracked in 3-month, 6-month, and 1-year increments. Analyze "which evaluation axis predicted performance" and continuously improve the evaluation axis.

Interview formats to avoid

Stress Interview

Pressure interviews and hooking questions are justified to see the candidate's “true personality under stress”, but in reality they only worsen the candidate experience and increase the rejection rate of the best.

Lengthy consecutive interviews

Six to eight hours of consecutive interviews per day can lead to candidate fatigue and poor interviewer judgment. It is recommended to divide it into multiple days.

Long-term effects

Organizations that conducted thorough structured interviews reported a 30-50% reduction in mismatches after joining the company, and a 10-15% increase in retention rates a year after joining the company. You can expect a return that exceeds the short-term cost increase.

title.rendered: 採用面接の認知バイアスを取り除く構造化面接
How to Learn Organizations from Exit Interviews

content.rendered:
Leavers are one of the most valuable sources of information for an organization. Honesty that I could not say during my tenure, honest evaluation of the organization, and what should be improved - these are the places where the exit interviews are held. However, in many organizations, exit interviews are always a formality. In this article, we will explain the design of extracting information.

Traditional Exit Interview Issues

Question 1: Will my manager do it?

A retired person may have been in trouble with the same boss during his or her tenure. I don't really mean it.

Question 2: Implement on the day of retirement

Leavers are interested in the “next workplace” and cannot focus on looking back.

Question 3: The questions are superficial

“Why did you leave?” “What do you think of the company?” are abstract questions that do not elicit the true meaning.

Designing effective exit interviews

Principle 1: Implemented by third parties

HR or an external career consultant will be in charge of the interview. Your manager will not be present. There is a real truth that can only be said because it is a third party.

Principle 2: After the retirement decision, within 2 weeks before the retirement

Make it clear that you have no intention of staying and position it purely as “final feedback to the organization.” Ideally, the timing should be 2 to 1 weeks before the retirement date.

Principle 3: Concrete case-based questions

Rather than asking abstract questions, “In the last 3 months, have there been any episodes where 'I wanted to say this but I couldn't'?” “What would you like to change if you stayed in the organization?” to dig deeper.

7 questions you should always ask

1. What are the expectations and actual gaps when joining the company?
2. During your tenure, have you felt most valued?
3. During your tenure, what has been your most troubling experience?
4. How was your relationship with your manager?
5. Did 1: 1s work?
6. How did you feel about the speed and transparency of the company's decision making?
7. What was your decision to choose the following workplace?

Using the information we collect

Quarterly Pattern Analysis

Accumulate feedback from leavers and analyze patterns on a quarterly basis. "Multiple resignations for the same reason" is a strong signal.

Regular reporting to management

Report the aggregated results of the exit interview to management on a quarterly basis. We will create a mechanism to reflect raw voices in business decisions.

Track Improvement Actions

Track improvement actions for issues identified in exit interviews and share their progress within the organization. The “Voice of the Retiree Changes the Organization” experience also gives current members a sense of security.

Maintaining relationships as a retirement network

Rather than treating retirees as “terminating their relationship with the organization,” they maintain their relationship as “graduates.” A good retirement experience leads to long-term referrals, re-entry, and building a reputation in the industry.

Effect from a long-term perspective

Organizations that lead exit interviews to improvement experience a structural improvement in turnover in 3-5 years. As the “culture of listening to the voices of retirees” takes hold, it will be easier for incumbent members to draw out their true selves and improve the frankness of the entire organization.

title.rendered: 退職面談から組織を学ぶ方法
Applying Design Sprints to Organizational Change

content.rendered:
Design sprints, popularized by Google Ventures, are known as a way to validate product ideas in five days. Applying the same methodology to organizational change can dramatically accelerate long-lasting transformation projects. This article explains how to apply it.

Traditional Organizational Transformation Issues

A typical organizational transformation project will have a long-term span of 6 months to 1 year. As a result, they fall into many patterns of failure, such as the distraction of issues, the cooling of the heat of the parties involved, and the deterioration of the goal itself due to environmental changes.

5-Day Sprint Application

Day 1 (Mon): Understanding Problems and Organizing Hypotheses

A day to deepen everyone's understanding of the organizational challenges to be transformed. Sharing the results of stakeholder interviews, visualizing the current organizational chart and authority structure, and planning the root cause hypothesis.

Day 2 (Tue): Divergence of solutions and generation of diverse options

Remove constraints and generate a large number of possible solutions. Parallel consideration of organizational structure change proposals, process reform proposals, approaches to cultural change, etc.

Day 3 (Wed): Evaluate the proposal and determine the direction

Organize the previous day's proposal with an evaluation matrix (impact x feasibility). Decide the direction, including the management. Operated as a "deciding meeting" rather than a "deciding meeting".

Day 4 (Thu): Prototyping

Specify the selected direction to the "minimum implementation" level. Prototyping a new organizational chart, prototyping a new meeting body, drafting a new evaluation system, and more.

Day 5 (Fri): Small Test and Reflection

Pilot prototypes, limited to 1-2 teams. Gather immediate feedback and develop an implementation plan for the next 30 days.

Three Benefits of Application

Benefit 1: Keeping heat in short-term focus

By compressing it over 5 days, participants will be able to run without losing their focus and energy.

Advantage 2: Stakeholder recognition is unified

By sharing the same 5 days, all participants will have the same assumptions. It is difficult to cause discrepancies in perception in the later operation phase.

Benefit 3: Reduce risk with early testing

A change plan that took a year to make will avoid "not working in the field". Early detection of the gap with reality in the test on the fifth day.

Operational precautions

Participant Selection

Ideally 5-7 people. Too much of it is debatable. Balanced management, site managers, representatives of transformation target departments, and external perspectives (HR consulting, etc.).

Importance of Preparation

In order to make effective use of the five days, it is extremely important to prepare in advance (stakeholder interviews, collection of current data). It is worth taking 2-4 weeks to prepare.

Follow-up after 30 days

Review the measures decided in the sprint after 30 days. Keep track of “What were the test results?” and “What are you going to do in the next 30 days?”

Effect from a long-term perspective

Breaking a one-year span transformation project down into “5 + 30 day cycles” dramatically increases the speed and flexibility of change. This approach is likely to spread in the future as a powerful way to increase the success rate of organizational change.

title.rendered: デザインスプリントを組織変革に応用する
How to spot micro-attacks that interfere with psychological safety

content.rendered:
Explicit harassment is easily recognizable and there are mechanisms in place to deal with it. On the other hand, subtle behaviors called “microaggression” undermine the psychological safety of the organization while being overlooked. This article explains specific examples, discovery methods, and organizational responses.

What is a micro-attack?

Micro-attacks, whether intentional or unintentional, convey subtle disregard or bias towards specific attributes or individuals. Each one is small enough to be dismissed as "crazy" or "overthinking", but when it accumulates, it creates a serious psychological burden.

5 Typical Examples

1. Recap what you said

"In other words○○, what he wants to say is × ×," he summed up arbitrarily, and replaced it with something different from his intention. It is especially common among women and young people.

2. Steal ownership of ideas

Someone else (often an authority figure) re-presents the ideas someone puts forward at the meeting and treats them as their own. The original speaker will be erased.

3. Sorting work based on stereotypes

Assign specific types of work as a matter of course because they are "women", "foreigners", and "young people". It will be done without confirmation of the person's intention.

4. Say “not like you”

"It's a decision unlike yours." "I thought you would do X", and binds the opponent's behavior with a frame of "likeness". It interferes with personal change and growth.

5. Interrupting or interrupting the discussion

Unconsciously interrupts the speech of a specific speaker (a member of a specific attribute). The cumulative difference in the amount of words spoken dilutes the person's presence within the organization.

3 Ways to Discover

Method 1: Measure the amount of speech in the meeting

Utilize tools that record the time and number of individual remarks at meetings (functions such as Zoom, dedicated SaaS). Signs of a structural problem if members of a particular attribute are consistently underspoken.

Method 2: Set up an anonymous feedback channel

Provide a monthly space where you can anonymously write "Recently Feeling Uncomfortable". By collecting specific examples, you can see the pattern.

Method 3: In-depth Exit Interview

Ask the retiree what he or she could not say during his or her tenure. The experience of micro-attacks is often finally told in retirement.

3 Steps to Organizational Responses

Step 1: Knowledge Sharing

Share micro-attack concepts and specific examples in manager training. There is no improvement where there is no “awareness”.

Step 2: Immediate call rule

Rules for gently pointing out micro-attacks on the spot. Correct it in such a way that "the way it is said may be conveyed differently than intended", while avoiding conflict.

Step 3: Institutionalize a place for reflection

Once a quarter, create a space for your team to share “What micro-attacks have you observed recently?” Treat it as a pattern learning, not a personal attack.

Long-term perspective

Dealing with micro-attacks is a three-year, long-term investment. While short-term results are difficult to see, they provide a solid improvement in retention and diversity metrics for top talent. It is the foundation for increasing organizational resilience.

excerpt.rendered: Unlike explicit harassment, “micro-attacks” quietly undermine psychological safety. Organize specific examples, discovery methods, and organizational approaches.

title.rendered: 心理的安全性を阻害するマイクロ攻撃を発見する方法
Complete guide to designing onboarding in 4 stages

content.rendered:
Organizations that view onboarding as a “first day of orientation” struggle with early departure of new hires. Originally, onboarding was an ongoing process that lasted from before joining the company until six months later. In this article, we will organize the 4-step frame and the implementation of each stage.

4-step frame overview

Phase 0: Pre-boarding. Phase 1: Day 1 to 30 (foundation phase). Phase 2: Day 31-90 (Initial Contribution Period). Phase 3: Day 91 ~ 180 (self-propelled establishment period). Goals and measures differ at each stage.

Phase 0: Pre-boarding

Objectives

Reduce anxiety before joining the company and create a smooth start on the first day of joining the company.

Specific measures

Send a welcome message one week before joining the company. Invite to internal tools (Slack, etc.) before joining the company (in observation mode). Send a video introducing yourself from a team member. Prepare the required device accounts in advance.

Phase 1: Day 1 - 30 (Foundation Stage)

Objectives

Adapting to the environment, building relationships, and mastering organizational culture. Business contribution is not a top priority.

Specific measures

Day 1: Welcome meeting, confirmation of required tool account setup. Day 2-14: 1: 1 with 10 or more key members. Day 15-30: Experience “how the organization works” with small tasks. Discover problems early with weekly check-ins.

THINGS THEY AVOID

Pack information on the first day (can't handle it), ask for results as an immediate force (leads to early disappointment), and formally assign a mentor (meaningless without substantive contact).

Phase 2: Day 31-90 (Initial Contribution Period)

Objectives

Reach a state where you can be responsible for one business area independently. Recognized as a force in the organization.

Specific measures

Clear assignment of areas of responsibility (narrowing them down to create a successful experience). Weekly 1: 1 progress check and removal of obstacles. 30/60/90 checkpoints to match expectations. Consciously create a horizontal connection with synchronous employees.

Phase 3: Day 91 - 180 (Self-Propelled Establishment Period)

Objectives

Achieved self-propelled state without detailed instructions from manager. When mid- to long-term commitment to an organization grows.

Specific measures

Expanding the area of responsibility, providing mentoring opportunities for juniors, participating in cross-organizational projects, and "reviewing the past and planning for the next six months" at the evaluation interview after six months.

3 failure patterns

1. Focus on the first day

The first day is packed too much, and the newcomer is exhausted. The first 30 days are divided by the "adaptation period".

2. Formal mentors

Satisfied with Assigned Mentor. It is meaningless without substantial contact frequency. Minimum of 30 minutes of conversation once a week.

3. Omit the 3-month retrospective

Imagine that you are already on your own, and skip the third month's reflection. In fact, this timing is a turning point in the establishment of the organization.

Quantitative effects

Organizations that implemented 4-step onboarding have seen a 15-25% increase in retention one year after hire and a 30% or more increase in performance attainment six months after hire. The return on investment is clear.

excerpt.rendered: Onboarding is not a “first day orientation” but an ongoing process over the first six months, with an implementation template explaining the design frame for the four phases (Pre/Day 1-30/Day 31-90/Day 91-180).

title.rendered: オンボーディングを4段階で設計する完全ガイド
Clarify Product Manager and EM roles

content.rendered:
Surprisingly, many organizations have a vague division of roles between Product Managers (PMs) and Engineering Managers (EMs). If the boundaries between the two are unclear, the team will be confused and neither of them will be able to focus on their role. This article introduces the clarification framework.

The Essential Difference

PM Areas of Responsibility

Responsible for deciding "what to make" and "why to make". Understand customer challenges, analyze market opportunities, prioritize, and develop roadmaps. Accountable for product success (business metrics).

EM Areas of Responsibility

Responsible for optimizing “how to make” and “who makes”. Team composition, technical judgment, development process, and development of members. Take responsibility for the health of your team and the quality of your output.

Common duplication patterns

Pattern 1: Both parties are involved in developing the roadmap

The PM decides what to do by when, but the EM also has a roadmap of what to do technically. Priorities collide.

Pattern 2: Both direct the work of team members

If the PM does "A, this is a request" and the EM also does "A, this is a request", the member will have two supervisors and be confused.

Pattern 3: Unclear evaluation authority

It is unclear whether PMs or EMs take the initiative in evaluating members. Cases where both parties comment but ultimate responsibility is unclear.

Clarification framework (applying RACI)

Separation of R (Responsible) and A (Accountable)

Document who is R and who is A for each decision area. Example: "Function Prioritization" is R = PM, A = PM. "Technical architecture judgment" is R = EM, A = EM. "Quarterly roadmap" is R = PM, A = PM, C (consultation) = EM.

Operational rules

Review matrixed RACIs semi-annually. The optimal allocation will change due to changes in the business phase and organization, so it will not be fixed.

Tips for working with PMs and EMs

1. Weekly sync sessions

30 minutes is fine, so PM and EM have time to synchronize regularly. Check priorities, share risks, coordinate next week's moves, and more.

2. Have common OKRs

Set one common metric that measures both the product and the team, as well as individual metrics. This is the centripetal force of the integration.

3. Unify communication with members

Teams get confused when PMs and EMs send different messages. Important things will be rubbed together in advance by two people, and the matched content will be sent out.

Avoid unique hybrid roles

Some organizations create a “PM/EM double”, but this often fails. It is virtually impossible to take on both responsibilities alone, with the result that both are halfway through. Even if your organization is small, we encourage you to have a clear division of responsibilities.

Effect from a long-term perspective

Organizations with clear roles make it easier for members to “consult with whom” and for work to flow smoothly. Vague organizations, on the other hand, continue to waste time on minor adjustments. An initial investment in clarification yields significant returns over the long term.

excerpt.rendered: Blurring the boundaries between PMs and EMs creates confusion and waste for the team. Describes the essential differences between the roles of the two, common overlapping patterns, and implementation frameworks for clarity.

title.rendered: プロダクトマネージャーと EM の役割分担を明確にする
Three design principles that create a sense of satisfaction with the evaluation system

content.rendered:
The evaluation system has a greater impact on the organization when it is "satisfactory" than when it is "accurately measured". An unsatisfactory rating system can cause talented people to leave first. In this article, we'll outline three principles that make you feel good.

Components of Satisfaction

Satisfaction consists of three things: transparency of the process, clarity of the criteria, and quality of the conversation. Satisfaction with the process leading up to the evaluation, not the absolute value of the score, determines the final acceptance.

Principle 1: Document and share the evaluation axis in advance

Background

“Why this assessment?” is a black box that doesn't convince me. It is assumed that the evaluation axis is shared at the beginning of the evaluation period, rather than being told after the fact.

Implementation

Summarize the “Expected Behavior/Outcome” for each position/grade in a 1-2 page document. Check this 1: 1 at the beginning of the evaluation period. Regular 1: 1 referrals during the semester minimize surprises to the assessment.

Principle 2: Calibration Between Managers

Background

Blurring the metrics for each manager undermines fairness across the organization. If “A-manager is loose” or “B-manager is harsh” occurs, trust in the evaluation will be undermined.

Implementation

Managers conduct calibration meetings in the middle and end of the evaluation period. Bring in each member's evaluation proposal to discuss and adjust the variation of the criteria. This is the core of the system and should not be omitted.

Principle 3: Feedback Focuses on Growing Next

Background

Appraisal interviews, which consistently explain past performance, are only painful for members. Focusing on “what you can do next” increases both satisfaction and motivation.

Implementation

The time allocation for the evaluation interview will be 30% of the past review, 20% of the current situation analysis, and 50% of the direction for the next half of the year. Simply focusing on the latter can make a big difference in the interview experience.

3 Operationalizing the Principles

From biannual evaluation interview to "quarterly check-in + biannual overall evaluation"

The longer the interval, the greater the "surprise". Short check-ins make the final assessment more predictable.

Separate appraisal and raise arguments

If the evaluation results are directly linked to the salary increase, you will not be able to discuss the real issue in the evaluation interview. The separation of the evaluation interview as a "growth conversation" and the salary increase is notified separately administratively is effective.

Review the assessment process itself annually

We will conduct an annual member survey on the evaluation system and continue to improve the process. There is no completed evaluation system.

Valuation system as a long-term investment

It takes more than three years of continuous investment to create a satisfactory evaluation system. But once established, it has a positive impact on all of our retention, engagement and recruiting brands. It should be positioned as an investment in long-term competitiveness rather than a short-term cost.

excerpt.rendered: A member's satisfaction with the rating system is directly linked to the organization's engagement. We will explain the three principles common to highly satisfactory evaluation systems and examples of operational implementation.

title.rendered: 評価制度の納得感を生む3つの設計原則
6 Practices to Build Trust with Remote Teams

content.rendered:
When I was working face-to-face, I would pass in the hallway, have lunch together, and laugh with each other in small talk - these "unintentional accumulations" naturally grew trust. In a remote environment, these will disappear and trust will need to be intentionally designed. In this article, we'll look at six proven practices.

The Three Components of Trust

Researchers define trust as consisting of three layers: trust in competence, trust in integrity, and trust in compassion. In a remote environment, “trust in compassion” is particularly difficult to grow and requires deliberate intervention.

6 Practices

1. Send a short video message once a month

The expression and tone of voice that are not conveyed in the text make the personality of the other person three-dimensional. It only takes 3 minutes for your manager to send you “Congratulations” and “Recent Concerns”.

2. Spend the first 5 minutes of your 1: 1 time talking outside of work

“How was your weekend?" What movies have you seen recently? "" and enter a light dialogue such as ". Trust is based on time that looks like a business' waste '.

3. Recognize members' birthdays and milestones as an organization

Signals that treat individuals as “one person” rather than “doers.” It works well with Slack's bot settings.

4. Blame “how it works” instead of “the individual” when it fails

Demonstrate a manager's willingness to take the initiative and treat failure as a “mechanics issue” when it occurs. This leads to the recognition that you can challenge with peace of mind here.

5. Make sure you keep your promises (or communicate changes early)

I will always do what I said, "I'll check it out by next week." Communicate the situation early if you are unable to do so. The accumulation of small promises is the accumulation of trust.

6. Disclose your weaknesses

Your manager's openness to saying, “I'm worried too,” or “I don't know,” brings out your team's candor. It takes courage not to play perfection.

Supporting design as an organization

As an organization that does not rely solely on individual efforts, we will maintain the following: Formulation of chat channels, monthly "get to know each other" sessions, how to visualize gratitude (Praise Bot, etc.), etc. These are not “if only”, they are essential infrastructure for remote organizations.

Long-term perspective

Building trust is not achievable in a short period of time. With 3-6 months of continuous practice, the atmosphere of your team will gradually begin to change. By the end of the year, you should have developed a trusting relationship that is close to face-to-face. It is important to cultivate intentionally because it is remote, not because it is remote.

excerpt.rendered: How can "trust" that naturally grows in person be created remotely? As we spend less time together, we'll walk you through six practices that intentionally foster trust and the organizational design that supports them.

title.rendered: リモートチームで信頼を構築する6つの実践
Bottom-Up Adoption: Field-Led Gathering Mechanism

content.rendered:
Traditional recruitment, where HR creates a pool of candidates and the field chooses them, has reached its limit. The shift to site-driven "bottom-up adoption" is spreading, especially in engineering organizations. In this article, we will organize the steps of the transition and the new role of HR.

Limitations of Traditional Adoption

In HR-driven, there are structural issues such as gaps in skill understanding with the field, differences in temperature perception during the wooing phase, and mismatches after joining the company. Especially in highly specialized occupations, there are too many things that can only be judged in the field.

4 Steps to Bottom-Up Recruitment

Step 1: Field members update the referral shortlist on a quarterly basis

Each member maintains a quarterly list of who they would like to work with, creating a permanent pool of 10-20 candidates. The list includes your current job, skills, wooing difficulty, and contact status.

Step 2: On-site members take the stage for the recruiting event

Senior members on-site will be actively responsible for taking the stage at external events, conferences, and study sessions. As a result, the “face of the company” seen by the candidate becomes a field member, reducing the psychological distance when applying.

Step 3: Always start the casual meeting with the on-site member

A member of your prospective team will be your first point of contact, rather than your HR counterpart. Candidates are more interested in deep technical discussions and realistic ways of working.

Step 4: Site Manager Involved in Final Adjustment of Offer Terms

The manager to be assigned will participate in the final determination of salary, position and assignment. The signal "I want the field to be serious" is transmitted to the candidate, and the effect of stalking is enhanced.

Changes in HR roles

The role of HR will change from "the main body of recruitment" to "creating a mechanism for recruitment activities".

Before Change: Hiring Executor

HR proactively performs candidate sourcing, screening, interview coordination, offer notification, etc.

After Change: Recruiting Enablers

Establish an environment in which the site can engage in recruitment activities. Implementation of referral tools, operation of candidate management SaaS, interviewer training, analysis and provision of recruitment data, etc.

Operational design supported as an organization

1. Justify recruiting time as work

Explicitly recognizes the time spent by site members on hiring as "work". Effort will be secured in the form of "10 hours per month of recruitment activities".

2. Incorporate hiring outcomes into the assessment

Evaluate contributions to referral success, interviewers, and candidate wooing. Without this, no one will spend any time.

3. Develop Candidate Pool Management Tool

A mechanism for storing candidate information as an organization rather than a personal note. Maintains relationships as organizational knowledge while respecting privacy.

Precautions during migration

If you suddenly make a full migration, it will fail. Start with one team, one role, and gradually expand after 3-6 months of effect validation. Learning from failure is the key to long-term retention.

Effect from a long-term perspective

Organizations with established bottom-up adoption tend to have fewer post-employment mismatches, higher referral rates, and lower hiring costs. Short-term operating costs increase, but long-term ROI is a high investment.

excerpt.rendered: HR-led hiring has its limits. We will explain in detail the four steps for the site to proactively gather candidates, select them, and build a persuasive mechanism, as well as the changes in HR roles and the operational design that supports the organization.

title.rendered: ボトムアップ採用：現場主導で人を集める仕組み
3 Timelines to Handle 1: 1

content.rendered:
When one-on-one 30 minutes consistently end with “the state of our work in the last week,” we lose much of our original value. By consciously allocating three timelines, one-on-one can support both short-term results and long-term growth. This article explains the role and operation of each axis.

3 timelines

Axis 1: Last week (removal of work obstructions)

The challenges that are happening in the field right now, the support that is needed, the priorities for next week, etc. Topics that are highly immediate. It is a place where managers understand the work of members and provide the support they need.

Axis 2: Last 3 months (project direction and skill development)

The medium-term outlook of the project, the arrival point as a skill, the learning opportunities required, etc. A place to share and coordinate "what will be achieved during this period" from a medium-term perspective.

Axis 3: Semi-Annual to 1 Year (Career and Role Dialogue)

A place to discuss what kind of career the person is aiming for, what role they want to experience, and what the organization can do to achieve this. It works for both long-term rapport and prevention of turnover.

Estimated Distribution

It doesn't have to be even every time. For weekly 1: 1, the distribution is realistic: Axis 1 is main (70%), Axis 2 is moderate (25%), and Axis 3 is small (5%).

Be sure to include the topic of "Axis 3" once a month

Deliberately set aside time for long term career conversations at the first or last 1: 1 of the month. If you make this a habit, members will also have the habit of thinking about what they will say towards the Axis 3 conversation next month.

Tips for making each axis work

Axis 1 Tip: “What can I do to support you?" every time you listen to it.

The manager's proactive approach to asking “what can be done” increases the member's psychological safety.

Tip for Axis 2: Grind the ideal state after 3 months

Check back every month to see what you want to be able to do in 3 months. This keeps your day-to-day work connected to your long-term goals.

Axis 3 Tips: Separate from evaluation interviews

When you combine career conversations with evaluation interviews, it's hard to be honest. It operates as an independent "place for career dialogue".

3-Axis Awareness Effects

Short-term effects

Improve productivity by eliminating business obstacles early.

Medium-term effects

The direction of skill development becomes clearer and members' autonomous learning behaviors increase.

Long-term effects

Engagement and retention increases when members feel that their organization is taking their careers seriously. One of the strongest players in the prevention of turnover.

Deployment Stages

Since it is difficult to suddenly be aware of all three axes, we will start by including Axis 3 (long-term career dialogue) once a month. After three months, members' attitudes will change, and the quality of Axis 2 and Axis 1 dialogue will naturally improve.

excerpt.rendered: If the 1: 1 agenda is skewed toward “most recent work,” long-term growth and building trust will be delayed. Organize the three timelines to be aware of and how to practice incorporating the themes to be addressed on each axis, the allocation guidelines, and the long-term perspective dialogues.

title.rendered: 1on1 で扱うべき「3つの時間軸」
Interpretation of the Edmondson Survey to Measure Psychological Safety

content.rendered:
The seven-point psychological safety survey developed by Amy Edmondson is widely used in research and practice. However, if you use it as it is in a Japanese company, you will experience a unique bias. In this article, we will organize how to interpret the seven items, correct for bias, and combine them with behavioral data.

Edmondson Survey 7 Items (Summary)

1. When I make mistakes on this team, I get blamed a lot.
2. Members of this team can bring up difficult issues.
3. Members of this team do not admit to being different.
4. It is very safe to take risks with this team.
5. It's hard to get help from other members of this team.
6. No one on this team is deliberately trying to demean me.
7. My unique skills and talents are appreciated when working with people on this team.

Three biases specific to Japanese companies

Bias 1: “Near-center response”

Japanese response patterns tend to gather in the center (3), avoiding extreme evaluations (1 and 5). This dilutes both positive and negative signals.

Bias 2: Difficulty with supervisors

In the case of an anonymous ceremony, it is difficult to give a negative answer to your boss. Data reliability is significantly reduced if anonymity is not fully assured.

Bias 3: “Unspeakable” doesn't answer

The most outspoken organization, the survey is less genuine. A paradoxical situation occurs where an organization with a high score is not necessarily “really safe.”

4 Ways to Read Corrections

1. Look at the amount of change in time series rather than absolute scores

Comparisons such as "3.8 vs. industry average 4.0" are meaningless. Track how it has changed compared to six months ago in the same organization.

2. Be sure to use the free description field together

Complement qualitative information with free text that cannot be seen with quantitative scores alone. Open-ended questions asking, "Specifically, when did you feel?" are effective.

3. Look at intra-departmental distribution rather than inter-departmental comparisons

Individual differences may be large even in the same department. You can find hidden problems by looking at the distribution of scores (standard deviation) as well as the average score.

4. Combine with behavioral data

Check against observable behavioral data, such as frequency of failure reports, percentage of dissenting meetings, and 1: 1 utterances. If the survey score and behavior do not match, it is a sign that something is distorted.

Read surveys by variation

Rather than competing for absolute scores, the attitude of tracking the amount of change in your organization is the biggest key to making the survey work. The essence is a cycle that hypothesizes why the score went up and why it went down, leading to the next start.

Operational precautions

The frequency of the survey is estimated to be once a quarter. Monthly implementation creates answer fatigue, and you can't keep up with changes once a year. The results will also be shared across the organization and publicly available improvement actions will build trust in the survey.

excerpt.rendered: Edmondson's 7-item survey is widely used, but results should be interpreted with caution. Organize biases specific to Japanese companies and how to read them to correct them. We will explain how to combine it with behavioral data, not just surveys alone.

title.rendered: 心理的安全性を測る Edmondson サーベイの解釈
Operational design to avoid making OKRs a “ritual”

content.rendered:
OKRs were originally designed to “align the organization's focus with ambitious goals,” but in many organizations this has become a “quarterly ritual.” Achievement of 100% is natural, non-achievement is blamed, and the goal is an extension of daily work - these are the opposite of the original intention of OKRs. In this article, we'll explain how to fix it.

The Three Symptoms of Ritualization

Symptom 1: 100% is taken for granted

Originally, OKRs are a mechanism for setting ambitious goals where 60-70% of achievement is healthy. Organizations that continue to achieve 100% are evidence that the goal is too low. However, when directly linked to the evaluation system, 100% becomes the "minimum line" and gradually moves towards a conservative goal setting.

Symptom 2: Setup session becomes “format”

The goal-setting session at the beginning of the quarter is just a ritual to glue past extensions. It is no longer a place to discuss “unprecedented goals” in earnest.

Symptom 3: Retrospection becomes acquittal

The review at the end of the quarter will be "explanation of why it was not achieved", and it will not lead to the next learning. All you have to do is spend time on a formal retrospective.

3 Design Changes to Rebuild

Change 1: Redefine 70% attainment as “healthy”

Repeatedly delivered by management, the message "100% achievement is evidence that the goal is too low" creates a culture that welcomes 70% achievement. This brings the courage to set truly ambitious goals back into the organization.

Change 2: Objective is ambitious and Key Results are measurably strictly separated

O (purpose) is only qualitative and indicates the direction of the organization. KRs (performance indicators) are strictly measurable numbers. Tightening this separation will change the quality of the discussion.

Change 3: Position the monthly progress meeting as a place of "course correction"

Review progress on a monthly basis instead of quarterly retrospectives. It is important to discuss not "how many points were scored" but "whether the direction is correct" and "whether it is necessary to correct the trajectory according to environmental changes".

Root Causes of Ritualization

When OKRs are directly linked to the evaluation system, they are reliably ritualized. If it is directly linked to evaluation, people can only set "achievable goals". This is a natural human reaction and a matter of institutional design.

Principle of separation of training, strategy and evaluation

OKRs = alignment of strategy

OKRs are strategic tools that align what you focus on as an organization.

1: 1 = nurture

Personal growth is 1: 1. A place to discuss one's career aspirations and learning opportunities rather than the progress of OKRs.

Evaluation System = Alternative Mechanism

Evaluations operate independently of OKRs and 1: 1s. See your overall contribution in one or two assessment cycles per year.

Effect from a long-term perspective

Once these segregations are achieved, OKRs begin to function as inherently ambitious goal-setting tools. The organization enters a “fear of failure” mode, resulting in a drive towards greater results. The total amount of long-term ambition is more competitive than the short-term achievement rate.

excerpt.rendered: OKRs became a "ritual" to set goals and measure achievement every quarter. Specific measures to rebuild operations that deviate from the original intention, the fundamental factors of ritualization, and the principle of separation from the evaluation system will be explained in detail.

title.rendered: OKR を「儀式」にしないための運用設計
3 Japanese practices avoided by Nordic startups

content.rendered:
Observing modern startups in Northern Europe, we find that Japanese companies consciously exclude customs that are considered "courtesy" and "consideration". Each has a reasonable reason. This article explains three practices and the ideas behind them.

3 Habits to Avoid

Habit 1: Calling by job title

In principle, it is not called "CEO X" or "Manager X", but only First Name. Internal emails are also based on "Hi John", and titles are mentioned only when necessary. This is not just a cultural difference, but a deliberate implementation to equalize the weight of speech in the discussion.

Practice 2: Formal Honorifics

Excessive respectful language is thought to hinder frank communication of intentions. In meetings, it is recommended to directly disagree with “I think you're wrong because…”, and it is not considered rude to do so. The assumption is shared that opposition to an idea is not an attack on personality.

Practice 3: Implicit decision-making authority by job title

Explicitly document the hierarchical distribution of authority, “This is up to the department manager” and “This is up to the section manager.” Be transparent about who decides what and prevent titles from shifting the discussion.

Background on why you should avoid

These are not implemented to produce "flatness", but to "do not lower the temperature of the discussion" and "do not determine the weight of opinions by position". We intentionally exclude things that we should avoid culturally as a prerequisite for a flat organization to function.

Tips for Japanese companies

You don't have to imitate it.

Even if you decide to "prohibit job titles", it often does not work in the cultural context of Japan. It's important to understand why you're avoiding it.

Translate

For example, we will introduce the rule that "I speak as the person in charge of the project, not the position at the meeting". Even if the job title continues to be called, we will devise ways to ensure that the job title does not distort the discussion in the discussion.

Change step by step

Rapid cultural changes create backlash. It is realistic to start small and expand it, such as "unifying the name in the management meeting" and "trying flat operation in a specific project".

See the rationale behind the practice

When looking at cross-cultural practices, focus on the rationality behind them, not the superficial form. Once you understand why they do what they do, you'll be able to translate according to your company's context. Rather than imitating the Nordic model as it is, the attitude of translating it into your own style is the key to successful long-term organizational change.

Effect from a long-term perspective

The review of these practices will affect the quality and speed of the organization's decision making over a three to five year span. In the short term, it may feel "difficult", but in the long term, it is an investment that directly leads to the competitiveness of the organization.

excerpt.rendered: Some common practices in Japanese companies are consciously avoided by Nordic startups. We will introduce three representative examples, the ideas behind them, and practical application tips for Japanese companies.

title.rendered: 北欧スタートアップが避ける3つの日本的慣習
Protocol design for asynchronous communication

content.rendered:
Simply saying “please use asynchronous communication” doesn't actually work. What should be documented, what should be the deadline for a response, and where to decide should be agreed in advance. This article outlines how to design an asynchronous protocol that works.

The four elements of the basic protocol

Element 1: The question is a three-point set of "background, current situation, desired judgment"

"What do you think of this? Ambiguous questions such as" are prohibited. Be sure to set rules that clearly indicate the three points: why you are listening (background), what kind of situation you are in (current situation), and what kind of judgment you are seeking (hope). This alone dramatically increases the accuracy and speed of your responses.

Element 2: 3 levels of urgency

Make it a rule to label all messages with urgency. Instant (within 30 minutes), within 24 hours, and within a week. This makes it easier for recipients to prioritize.

Element 3: Clearly separate unresponsive notifications and requests for judgment

Use prefixes such as [FYI], [Action Required], and [Decision Needed]. Make recipients' interactions clearer and reduce unnecessary trips.

Element 4: Operation of "Silence = Consent"

Use the format “If there is no objection within 24 hours, we will proceed with this proposal.” Waiting for everyone's explicit consent will stop the judgment. Operations that consider silence to be consent maintain asynchronous speed.

Tool Selection Perspective

Strict use of Slack/Teams threading

One agenda in one thread. Discipline that is not scattered in the channel creates a state where you can follow up on the discussion later.

Agenda-based aggregation in Notion/Confluence, etc.

A document-based discussion platform is useful if you need a long-term discussion or a compilation of opinions from multiple people. Comment asynchronously.

Video messages such as Loom

It may be faster to send a 3-5 minute video with a longer description in writing. Hybrid operation with characters is modern.

Antipattern

1. Make important decisions with Slack

If you make a decision with a tool that flows and disappears, you will not be able to follow it later. Decisions are always documented.

2. CC everyone

As more people get involved, no one will read it. The discipline of carefully selecting "only those who should read".

3. Expect an immediate response

A culture that touts asynchrony but seeks essentially instant replies is the worst kind of hybrid. Once asynchronous, we will create a waiting system.

The Road to Fixation

Asynchronous cultures don't take hold overnight; they require 3-6 months of continuous development and leadership from managers. At first, you may feel that synchronized meetings are easier, but in the long run, there will be a big difference in the mobility of the organization.

excerpt.rendered: Saying “proceed asynchronously” in a remote environment won't work without explicit rules. It explains how to design basic protocols for asynchronous communication, tool selection, and even antipatterns.

title.rendered: 非同期コミュニケーションのプロトコル設計
5 Signs of Flat Tissue Collapse

content.rendered:
While flat organizations are talked about as ideals, implementation is difficult, and many fall into tacit hierarchies and stagnant decision-making. Early detection of pre-collapse signs is the key to making flat tissue work. In this article, we'll go over the signs and workarounds.

5 signs that appear before the collapse

Sign 1: Described as a “natural” decision

It is unclear who made the decision and how. In fact, it is decided by a certain influential person, but it is not verbalized. When the word "we all decided" starts to appear, it is a red flag.

Signal 2: Influencers and Titles Diverge

There is a big difference in the substantive influence of the position, even if it is equal. Structures that do not reflect the opinions of newcomers or minorities are fixed.

Sign 3: New hires take more than 3 months to catch up

Because there are many implicit rules and implicit permission structures and they are not documented, it takes time for newcomers to understand the organization. This is a serious sign.

Signal 4: The same argument is repeatedly distilled

The same point is repeated because there is no record of the decision and it is unclear who decided what. The state in which the learning of the organization does not accumulate.

Indication 5: Extremely difficult to take over in retirement

They rely too much on tacit knowledge, so when people quit, their work stops. Evidence that organizational robustness depends on the individual.

3 Principles of Workaround

Principle 1: Document roles and responsibilities

Make it clear who is responsible for which area, even if it is flat. This is different from creating a hierarchy. A flat discussion is only possible if there is clarity about where the responsibility lies.

Principle 2: Visualize the decision-making process

Adopt explicit decision making protocols, such as “advisory processes” and “outlet methods.” Document who consults whom and ultimately who decides.

Principle 3: Enhance onboarding design for new hires

We will consciously document tacit knowledge and aim to make it readable and self-propelled by newcomers. We will create a mechanism to understand the structure of the organization in 3 months.

Flatness is not the absence of structure

Some managers misunderstand flat organizations as “unstructured organizations”, but this is a decisive mistake. Flatness is an advanced structure built on explicit design. Rather, it requires more investment in the design of document process communication than in hierarchical organizations.

Regular health checks

Once a quarter, make a habit of checking the health of your organization for the five signs listed above. If you can find it early, the correction cost will be small.

excerpt.rendered: It is common for a flat organization that began with ideals to become dysfunctional in six to two years. It explains the five signs and workarounds that appear before a collapse, and the underlying principle that flatness is an explicit design, not an absence of structure.

title.rendered: フラット組織が崩壊する5つの兆候
Write your hiring pitch deck in Stories

content.rendered:
A typical failure of a hiring pitch deck is to “end up with the numbers, benefits, and technology stack of the business side by side.” What candidates really want to know is the story of what they can be there for. In this article, we will organize the structure of the narrative pitch and the expressions that should be avoided.

It consists of four chapters

Chapter 1: The Real Problems We Want to Solve

We will talk about the social and industrial issues facing the company in specific episodes rather than abstract theories. The starting point of the story is that customers have these kinds of worries, and we want to change them. Instead of specs and numbers, it's important to have a narrative that reaches your emotions.

Chapter 2: Why This Team Is Doing It Now

With the background of the founders and key people, we will talk about “why we are working on this problem” and “why we are a member now”. From the perspective of "working with people" rather than "joining the company".

Chapter 3: What kind of work do you believe in?

Rather than a list of benefits, we linguize what we value and what we throw away as an organization. Explicit trade-offs like “We sacrifice YY to value XX” are persuasive.

Chapter 4: The Role Stories I Expect of You

Rather than a requirement such as "◯years of experience or more", it illustrates the◯◯ expected value on a timeline of "◯◯three months after joining the company◯◯, one year, and three years". Write at a granular level where candidates can imagine their future.

4 phrases to avoid

1. "Industry-leading XNUMX"

There is no specificity, anyone can write. Replace with specific numbers or examples.

2. “A rewarding job”

Subjective, not communicative. Show what is “rewarding” in a specific episode.

3. “Growth Environment”

I don't know who it is for or how it will grow. Demonstrate by specific learning opportunities and role changes.

4. “Homey vibe”

Too abstract. Describe in detail what everyday scenes are available (team lunches, chat culture, ease of consultation).

Common elements of a successful pitch deck

A good pitch deck creates a situation where a candidate who has finished reading “wants to tell someone about this company.” Stories can be shared. The pitch of the spec line is memorable to the candidate and does not generate word-of-mouth.

Regular updates

The pitch deck is not “make and finish” but a living document that should be reviewed every 3-6 months. As business phases and organizations change, the stories we tell evolve.

excerpt.rendered: The recruiting materials in the spec column do not move the candidate's mind. A good hiring pitch deck tells the “story” of your company. It provides an implementation guide that teaches you how to structure your 4 chapters, what to avoid, and what to learn from real-world success stories.
