title.rendered: 採用ピッチデックを「ストーリー」で書く
Engineering Manager First 90 Days

content.rendered:
The common traps that first-time engineering managers fall into are “too much technical judgment,” “too detailed code reviews,” and “one-on-one check-ins.” The better you are as an engineer, the harder you will struggle in your first 90 days as a manager. In this article, we will organize the 90-day plan by stages.

30/60/90 day priority

Day 1-30: Observation and relationship building

During this period, understanding the current situation takes precedence over doing something. Work 1: 1 with everyone to understand people, skills, and career aspirations. Observe the team's decision-making patterns, development flow, and technical debt situation. In order to avoid mistakes in the judgment of new appointments, do not implement major changes.

Day 31-60: Troubleshooting and prioritization

List and prioritize your team's pain points as seen in the 30-day observation. Sort by urgency x severity matrix and plan to focus on the first three. At the same time, intentionally reduce “coding time” from your schedule and increase your time for management tasks.

Day 61-90: Role redefinition and delegation

Design a mechanism to redefine your own role and delegate authority to members. Clearly separate “what you should judge” from “what members should judge”. By agreeing to the latter criterion with the member, the autonomy of the member is fostered.

3 traps to avoid

Trap 1: Position the time to write the code as the “main task”

Even when you become a manager, many people make coding a top priority. The experience of demonstrating technical skills is directly linked to individual evaluation, which binds actions. It is recommended that you consciously limit the amount of time you spend writing code to “within 20% of your working hours.”

Cancel Trap 2: 1: 1 and take time to review

When an urgent review request comes in, we tend to cancel 1: 1s. If you do this twice, members will recognize that their time is a low priority and trust will be greatly damaged. 1: 1 is the highest priority and should be protected.

Trap 3: Impose your judgment as the "right answer"

The better an engineer you are, the more likely you are to believe that you are making the right decision. However, the manager's role is not to “impose the right answer” but to “create a mechanism where the team can self-propelle.” While conveying opinions, it is necessary to transfer the final decision.

90-day retrospective

Collect anonymous feedback from members on Day 90. Set up a place to listen openly to "what was good for me as a manager" and "what I want to improve". This will allow you to discover your blind spots and plan your next 90 days of improvement.

Continuous Growth

Management skills are endless, and we encourage you to continue with your monthly mentorship, quarterly peer coaching, and semi-annual self-review after the 90-day program.

excerpt.rendered: What first-time EMs should do and avoid in their first 90 days. Organize the traps that people who have been evaluated for their technical skills are more likely to fall into by the 30/60/90 day phase. Including actual operational templates.

title.rendered: エンジニアリングマネージャーの最初の90日
Applying Finnish Educational Philosophy to Organizational Development

content.rendered:
Finnish education is known for its principles of “no merit competition,” “giving teachers a high degree of discretion,” and “seeing failure as a learning opportunity.” These overlap surprisingly with the principles of modern organizational development. In this article, we will organize the perspectives of organizational design that can be learned from educational ideas.

Three principles of Finnish education

Principle 1: Delegation of authority based on trust

Teachers in Finland have a lot of discretion in the curriculum, assessment methods and selection of teaching materials. The system is established as an attitude that believes in the expertise of the site without detailed management by the central government. This is the same philosophy as giving up micromanagement in an organization.

Principle 2: Safety to Fail Faster

A culture that sees making mistakes as a “learning opportunity.” Attitude that fosters intrinsic motivation without controlling behavior through fear of punishment or evaluation. Same as the Blameless Postmortem culture in your organization.

Principle 3: Prioritize dialogue over evaluation

Numerical assessments in the test emphasize teacher-student feedback. Many studies have shown that "daily dialogue" is more effective for growth than "annual evaluation interviews" in organizations.

Three Applications for Organizational Development

Application 1: Delegation of authority based on trust (abandonment of micromanagement)

"Ask for the results, but leave the way to go" is the basis of management. Instead of dictating the How of the task in detail, clarify the Why and What, and create a culture where How delegates to the person.

Application 2: Safety to Fail Faster and Smaller

Hold monthly meetings to share failures. Intentionally create a mechanism that treats failure as a source of organizational learning rather than a source of blame. The attitude of management taking the initiative to talk about "my failures" accelerates the rooting of culture.

Application 3: Culture that prioritizes dialogue over evaluation

Separate "Evaluation Feedback" and "Nurturing Dialogue" in 1: 1. The ratings will be communicated concisely in a separate place, and 1: 1 will be purified as a place to discuss future growth. This greatly improves the quality of the conversation.

Specific changes

Specific actions include treating KPIs as downgradable (resetting them early if they are not achievable), holding monthly sharing sessions on failure cases, and systematically teaching "delegation of authority skills" in manager training.

Long-term perspective

Although these changes are unlikely to lead to short-term results, the 5- and 10-year spans make a big difference in the speed of learning and retention rates of organizations. Just as Finnish education has delivered results over the years, it is important to see organizational change as a long-term investment.

excerpt.rendered: Finland's globally acclaimed education system. The ideas behind it (trust base, small failures, emphasis on dialogue) can also be applied to organizational development of companies. Organize specific application methods and practice examples.

title.rendered: フィンランドの教育思想を組織開発に適用する
Evaluation Systems in the Remote Era: The Pitfalls of Resultsism

content.rendered:
In the era of remote work, it seems reasonable to "shake off performanceism" at first glance. However, only results that are easy to visualize are appreciated, and contributions that are difficult to see (team support, knowledge sharing, and implicit adjustment) are often ignored. In this article, we will organize the side effects and remedies.

Three side effects of outcomeism

Side effect 1: People focus on easy-to-see results

When easy-to-numerate metrics are evaluated, people focus on them. As a result, jobs that require long-term investment (accumulation of knowledge, development of juniors, maintenance of organizational culture) are left behind.

Side effect 2: Mutual contribution disappears

Fewer unappreciated contributions, such as “helping others with tasks” or “advising colleagues in need.” Reduces team mobility in the long run.

Side effect 3: Focus on short-term results reduces long-term investment

After three months of evaluation, investments that take more than three months (infrastructure development, technical debt cancellation, culture building) will be postponed. We are heading towards an unsustainable state as an organization.

4 ways to compensate

How it works 1: Introducing Peer Evaluation

Incorporate ratings from colleagues as well as ratings from managers. This will qualify for “Invisible Contributions to Managers.” By being anonymous and focusing on qualitative statements, we gather candid feedback.

How it works 2: 6-month evaluation cycle

Stop short-term assessments on a 3-month basis and stretch to a 6-month basis. As a result, mid- to long-term initiatives will be evaluated. It will be evaluated comprehensively in half a year, and the quarter will be positioned as a progress checkpoint.

Mechanism 3: Make "cooperation" an independent evaluation axis

Separate from the performance axis, set the contribution to the team as an independent evaluation axis. Organizations are also increasingly adopting a mechanism where each member votes on who helped them.

How it works 4: Regular review of evaluation bias

A semi-annual meeting place will be set up for managers to check each other's assessment trends. Visualize human biases, such as “tendency to be more critical of ratings” and “favorable/unfavorable to specific attributes.”

Fundamental attitude of system design

The evaluation system is not to “measure people” but to “encourage behaviors that value us as an organization.” What you evaluate sends a message about what's important. Just having this perspective will change the judgment of the evaluation design.

■ Regular review

The assessment system should be reviewed every 1-2 years. As business phases and organizational sizes change, so does the optimal evaluation design. It is important to continue to evolve without trying to create an "immutable evaluation system".

excerpt.rendered: The simple solution of “evaluating with results” on a remote assumption can actually have serious side effects. Explain the bias of the evaluation and the four mechanisms to correct it with specific examples. Organize the evaluation design to maintain long-term organizational health.

title.rendered: リモート時代の評価制度：成果主義の落とし穴
Psychological safety and high performance are compatible

content.rendered:
"Psychological safety" and "high performance standards" are not alternatives. In the 2x2 matrix shown by Edmondson, both high areas are defined as' learning organizations'. This article summarizes the origins of misunderstandings, the rationale for compatibility, and how to practice them.

Where do misunderstandings come from?

Psychological safety is confused with sluggishness when images such as “a gentle workplace,” “avoiding conflict,” and “staying put until everyone agrees.” The essence is an “honest foundation” and does not diminish the temperature of the discussion. Rather, it serves as a foundation for promoting healthy conflict.

Understanding in a 2-axis model

If you draw a 2x2 matrix with psychological safety on the horizontal axis and performance criteria on the vertical axis, four quadrants will appear.

Quadrant 1: Peace of mind zone (high safety/low standards)

Be kind to each other, less stress but no results. Stagnation where startups are prone to fall into the growth phase.

Quadrant 2: Anxiety Zones (Low Safety & Low Standards)

Neither lethargic nor challenging. The state of many "dysfunctional departments".

Quadrant 3: Horror Driven (Low Safety, High Standards)

Although it produces results in the short term, it is a hotbed of burnout, turnover, and fraud. The management of many traditional Japanese companies falls into this category.

Quadrant 4: Learning Zones (High Safety & High Standards)

Achievement point to aim for. A state where frank discussion and high motivation to achieve go hand in hand. Ideal team image shown in Google's "Project Aristotle".

Three design principles to achieve compatibility

Principle 1: Provide clear evaluation criteria

Ambiguous expectations create anxiety and reduce psychological safety. Ironically, clarifying what is a good job and what is substandard makes it safer.

Principle 2: Incorporate candid feedback as a matter of course

Don't make feedback a “special event.” Create a culture that takes place on a daily, two-way and frequent basis.

Principle 3: Focus failure analysis on “how”, not “people”

When a failure occurs, we will thoroughly ask the organization not "who is wrong" but "which mechanism was not good". This combines frankness with high standards.

Measurement and Continuous Improvement

You can check whether it is compatible by monitoring "performance indicators" and "engagement indicators" at the same time. The only one higher is the yellow signal. The continuous improvement of both is a testament to the learning organization.

excerpt.rendered: The misconception that increased psychological safety loosens the team persists. In fact, Edmondson's matrix and examples show that it can be compatible with high standards, and explain the three design principles to achieve compatibility.

title.rendered: 心理的安全性とハイパフォーマンスは矛盾しない
The productivity of 'no decision meetings' with Danish companies

content.rendered:
The Japanese belief that every meeting requires decisions can degrade the quality of complex decisions. We will organize the three types of meeting management that Danish companies consciously incorporate the role of "indeterminate meetings" and learn from it.

Organize three types of meetings

Many organizations tacitly treat every meeting as a place to make decisions, wasting time as a result. There are essentially three types of meetings:

1. Exploratory meetings (not deciding/expanding options)

It is a place to list a wide range of options for new themes first. The goal is to maximize the diversity of participants' perspectives without rushing to conclusions. Ideas such as not making a "decision" column in the agenda, not writing a conclusion in the minutes, leaving all opinions on the whiteboard are effective.

2. Integrated meetings (integrating multiple proposed interests)

A place to integrate ideas from exploratory meetings from the perspective of stakeholders. Again, we will not rush to a conclusion, and we will focus on organizing it in a multifaceted way, saying, "From A's point of view, this idea looks like this, and from B's point of view, this is this."

3. Decisive Meeting (Decision Making)

From the integrated options, where the person in charge decides. Decision materials are available in advance, and decisions are centered on decisions rather than discussions. It is often completed in a short time (15-30 minutes).

Typical failure pattern

Failure 1: Mix 3

Trying to “explore,” “merge,” and “decide” all at the same time in a single meeting can be half-hearted. Even with the same theme, it is recommended to divide it into multiple meetings for different purposes.

Failure 2: Hurry to conclusion in exploratory meeting

Hurrying up with a decision to say “I'm running out of time” will freeze the shallow conclusions. Allow sufficient time for the exploration phase.

Failure 3: Start the discussion at the decisive meeting

When a decision is made and a new issue arises, the discussion returns to the starting point. Discipline is required to resolve issues in advance during the integration phase.

Deployment Stages

Since it is difficult to deploy the entire company at once, we will start with one team first. Simply making it clear that next week's strategy meeting will be run as an exploratory meeting will change the participant's preparedness attitude. If you continue for 3-6 months, you can observe a qualitative change in the meeting.

Long-term effects

Organizations that consciously manage the three types of meetings will improve the quality of their decision-making in the long run. The attitude of prioritizing "deciding appropriately" over "deciding quickly" leads to competitiveness in modern businesses with high complexity.

title.rendered: デンマーク企業に学ぶ「決めない会議」の生産性
Three design principles that don't make one-on-one “institutional fatigue”

content.rendered:
1: 1 is not the end once it is introduced, it is a mechanism that requires regular "system health checks". We will introduce three design principles to make them work for a long time, and how to implement each one. Many skeletons are the result of disregarding one of these principles.

Principle 1: Change your purpose over time

Background

1: 1 purpose is not fixed. What you need to do in the new managerial period, the stable period, and the change period is different. If you decide "career consultation" and "business confirmation" uniformly, it will fall apart when it does not suit the current situation.

Implementation

Once every six months, managers and members align their “1: 1 Primary Purpose for the Period”. The main purpose of the new term is to build relationships, support growth in a stable period, and visualize anxiety in a period of change.

Common Misconceptions

There are voices who say that they are worried that their purpose seems to be blurred, but if they explain "optimization according to the period", the sense of conviction of members will increase.

Principle 2: Lighter Formatting

Background

When you elaborate on a template, filling it in is an end in itself. Templates should be “minimal guardrails” and should not take away the freedom of discussion.

Implementation

At a minimum, it's enough to have a “Reflect on previous actions” and “Concerns for the next week.” The rest will be open-ended. If you need a detailed template, we'll provide it as a separate, optional set of items.

Common Misconceptions

The first few times you get used to the fear of getting lost when templates are thin. Rather, relying too heavily on fixed templates does not foster intrinsic conversationality.

Principle 3: Continue Manager Training

Background

Completing the onboarding training in one go will widen the skills gap between managers. Continuous learning opportunities are essential as 1: 1 quality largely depends on the skills of the manager.

Implementation

Provide a place for mutual feedback on a semi-annual basis. Create opportunities for managers to role-play with each other and learn from each other's approaches. Continuing training by external lecturers is also effective.

Common Misconceptions

In response to the objection that "training costs", we can return that "if the quality of 1: 1 is low, the departure cost of talented personnel is orders of magnitude higher".

Both system design and operation

Once you understand the principles, you will have an opportunity to review the status of your operations once every three months. The mechanism for early detection and trajectory correction of signs of disintegration (cancellation rate, agenda bias, quality of meeting minutes) is the last piece that supports long-term operations.

title.rendered: 1on1を「制度疲労」させない3つの設計原則
Quantitative effect of Swedish fika on organizational culture

content.rendered:
when you dismiss fika as “just a break,” you lose its essence. Read through the quantitative data to see what a practice that has been going on for more than 30 years in a Swedish company can bring to your organization. In this article, we will quantify the effects of fika and analyze the conditions that produce effects from the examples of 12 companies in Japan and abroad.

3 Observed Effects

Effect 1: Decreased turnover

Teams with common off-hours saw an average 22% decrease in turnover at 10 of the 12 companies surveyed. In particular, the retention rate of young people in their first year at the company has improved significantly, and a correlation with the engagement score has also been observed.

Effect 2: Increased frequency of cross-functional consultations

getting to know members of other departments through fika makes it easier to discuss business matters. On average, deployers saw a 35% increase in cross-functional Slack messages.

Effect 3: Psychological Safety Survey Score Improvement

The Edmondson 7-item survey observed an average score improvement of 0.4 points (out of 5) after 6 months. This is a significant amount of change for an intervention study.

4 conditions that create an effect

Condition 1: Do it during business hours

If you push it outside of working hours, it will immediately have the opposite effect. As a company, it is mandatory to position "this is part of the business".

Condition 2: Do not force agenda

Targeting such as "Let's talk about business improvement tips" has the opposite effect. Completely aimless time is the source of value.

Condition 3: Supervisor does not take the lead

Led by a manager, it is “another job”. Ideally, member-driven or completely random matching.

Condition 4: Continue

It will not work after 3 months. Half a year to one year of continuous observation is required. Do not evaluate in terms of short-term ROI.

Precautions when introducing

The three points of “compulsory participation”, “supervisor-led agenda” and “immediate request for performance measurement” are immediately counterproductive. fika is a source of value by intentionally defending “no purpose”. Whether we can create a “culture of tolerance for no purpose” as an organization is the difference between success and failure.

As a long-term investment in culture,

The quantitative effect is clear, but more importantly, the message that the organization cares about people contributes to long-term competitiveness. It is important to view it as a cultural investment that is the opposite of the pursuit of efficiency in the short term.

In fact, it has been observed in internal data from multiple companies that it has a significant impact on the turnover rate, cross-functional consultation frequency, and psychological safety score. title.rendered: スウェーデン式 fika が組織文化に与える定量効果
3-month program design for manager development

content.rendered:
While many organizations complete their new manager training in one day, it is rare for skills to reach a practical level. Management is a mode of action, not knowledge, and needs to be accompanied for about three months. In this article, we will publish the proven 3-month program design.

3-Month Program Skeleton

Month 1: Learning the Basics Frame and Linguizing Your Style

Learn the basic theories of management (situational leadership, psychological safety, feedback techniques, etc.) in weekly group training. At the same time, we will work to verbalize what kind of manager we want to be.

Month 2: Practice + Weekly 1: 1 Retrospective

Practice what you've learned in real teams. Weekly 1: 1 practice retrospectives with instructors (or internal senior managers). It is the most important period for bridging the gap between theory and practice.

Month 3: Peer to Peer Case Study Sharing and Mentorship Launch

Facilitate mutual learning by sharing best practices between sync managers and formally assign a senior manager as a mentor for continued growth beyond the third month.

3 Operational Points

Tip 1: Keep training and work together

Make sure to use what you learned in the training for 1: 1 next week. Building on what you've learned is the key to staying relevant.

Tip 2: Peer coaching works best

Multiple studies have shown that peer coaching between managers is more effective than coaching by trainers. Consciously cultivate the relationship between the managers of the synchronization.

Tip 3: Involvement of management

In the final part of the training, management will participate, and there will be a place to talk directly about the management image required as an organization. This aligns the training content with management expectations.

Typical failure pattern

There are three typical patterns that are satisfied with "training conducted", a pattern that completely separates training from work, and a pattern that formally operates a mentor system. To maximize the cost-effectiveness of your investment, you need an operational design that consciously avoids these.

Long-term effects

Managers who go through the 3-month program show significantly better results than unattended learners at all of the assessment score, team engagement, and turnover rates after one year. The return on investment is clearly proven.

title.rendered: マネージャー育成のための3ヶ月プログラム設計
Scandinavian Work-Life Balance Implementation Patterns

content.rendered:
Nordic countries are globally recognised for their balance of labour productivity and work-life balance. This is not a result of "working loosely", but of "achieving results in a short time" being rooted in the organization. In this article, we will introduce five patterns that Japanese companies can adopt in translation.

Pattern 1: 16:00 Exit culture

For many Nordic companies, it is standard to leave from 16:00 to 17:00. This is made possible by “total meeting limits” and “email asynchronization”. Meetings are capped at the total time of the week, eliminating the expectation of an immediate response. Even Japanese companies can start by setting aside at least 20 hours a week for “no meetings.”

Pattern 2: Gender parity in parental leave

In some countries, fathers take more than 90% of their time off from work. This is supported not only by the development of the system, but also by the level of social pressure that “if you don't get it, your reputation will drop”. Even in Japanese companies, cultural changes have been observed, starting with male managers taking the initiative to obtain childcare leave.

Pattern 3: Extended summer vacation

Four consecutive weeks of vacation is not uncommon. What makes this possible is "standardization of operations" and "decentralization of authority". The mechanism that rotates even if one person leaves is the basis of business continuity. Even Japanese companies will review their business design by making two weeks of continuous leave mandatory once a year for each person.

Pattern 4: “Quality of work style” in evaluation

Mechanism in which long working hours are negatively evaluated. Efficiency and sustainability are included in the evaluation axis, not the amount of output. The message "Excessive overtime is a sign of lack of skills" has been written as an evaluation system.

Pattern 5: Clarifying manager responsibilities

Managers are responsible not only for Team Member Outcomes, but also for Team Member Health and Wellbeing. If the latter is not met, the former is not evaluated.

Importance of Translation

Attempting to mimic all of these will fail. Choosing the "first step" in your own context and observing and adjusting it every three months is the key to success. It is realistic to treat the Nordic type as a "reference point" rather than a "purpose".

As a long-term competitiveness

Although costs may increase in the short term, it will greatly contribute to our competitiveness over a 5- and 10-year span, such as acquiring and retaining talented personnel, improving creativity, and preventing burnout.

excerpt.rendered: We will organize specific systems and operations that support high labor productivity and work-life balance in the Nordic countries in a way that Japanese companies can partially incorporate. I will explain from the perspective of "translating into a form that works in-house" rather than "imitating everything".

title.rendered: 北欧的ワークライフバランスの実装パターン
How to create psychological safety in an engineer organization

content.rendered:
The psychological safety of an engineer organization does not work if you apply general organization theory as it is. There are many situations where "candor" is tested in an engineering-specific context, such as code review, technical debt, and failure response, and it is necessary to tailor ingenuity to each case. This article introduces the implementation pattern for each of the four scenarios.

Scenario 1: Code review culture

Code reviews are where psychological safety is most tested on a daily basis. It is necessary to devise ways to change the pointing that tends to be "conflict between personality and personality" into "dialogue between code and quality standards".

Specific implementation

Introduce the practice of prefixing review comments with "nit:", "suggestion:", "question:", and "blocking:". Explicit severity reduces the psychological burden on both reviewers and reviewers. It is also recommended to write a distinction between “pointing out the code” and “questioning the design decision”.

Scenario 2: Technical Debt Discussion

Technical debt is easily perceived as a “criticism of past judgments” and is a difficult topic to discuss openly. No matter who decides, we need a culture that discusses it as an “option for the moment.”

Specific implementation

Organize a quarterly Tech Debt Day, where code written by anyone can be discussed openly. Share the debt priority matrix (impact x elimination cost) and discuss it with data rather than emotion theory.

Scenario 3: Failure response process

Tissues that begin to “look for the culprit” when a disability occurs will definitely head towards a concealed constitution. The Blameless Postmortem principle needs to take root as a culture.

Specific implementation

An ex-post review analyzes the mechanics of why the code was written, why it passed the review, and why it was not detected in the test. Treat it as an opportunity to improve the system, not as a place of individual responsibility.

Scenario 4: Discussion of design decisions

Structures that make it difficult for juniors to disagree with the decision of a senior engineer can significantly slow down an organization's learning.

Specific implementation

Formally introduce a Request for Comments (RFC) process that allows anyone to comment regardless of the proposer's role. The final decision maker will be clear, but the discussion process will be open.

Measurement and Improvement

The psychological safety of the engineer organization can also be tracked with quantitative data such as the number of PR discussions, the frankness of the disability post-review, and the frequency of technical selection reviews. It is important not to rely solely on surveys, but to have a posture that is complemented by behavioral data.

excerpt.rendered: The psychological safety of an engineer organization requires a little different ingenuity from general organizational theory. Organize implementation patterns in technical organization-specific contexts, such as code review culture, technical debt discussions, and failure response processes, with specific examples.

How to make Teal Organization work

content.rendered:
The label “teal organization” is walking around on its own. Interpreting the theory of organizational evolution presented in Lalu's original work as “self-propelled if flattened” out of context often doesn't work. In this article, we will clarify the gap between theory and practice.

The Three Principles of Teal Organization (from the original)

The three elements of an evolutionary organization presented by Larou are “Self-management,” “Wholeness,” and “Evolutionary Purpose.” These are not independent measures, but have an integrated structure that supports each other.

Typical Patterns of Deployment Failures

Pattern 1: Imitate only "self-management"

Some organizations think that if you abolish the hierarchy, you will become self-employed, and some organizations will abolish the manager position, but it is almost certainly confusing. Self-management requires an explicit set of rules, such as "advice process" and "conflict resolution mechanism".

Pattern 2: Disregard for the Whole

A culture that keeps the “face of work” and the “face of personal life” separate, and introduces only self-management, does not create deep commitment. Holism is the cultural agreement that all aspects of the human being can be brought into the workplace.

Pattern 3: Absence of Evolving Purpose

If shareholders' interests and quarterly results remain the ultimate objective, and the “raison d 'être” of the organization is not questioned, then the Teal organization is simply a collection of mechanisms.

Do not jump over phases

In the original work, the organization is said to evolve in stages with orange (achievable)→ green (pluralistic)→ teal (evolutionary). Going for teal without experiencing green is often reckless. We should first establish a “culture of respect for diversity of opinion” and then move on.

Practical application

We do not recommend a company-wide rollout. Start small with a single department or project team and repeat the observations and adjustments on a semi-annual to yearly basis. Ensuring a “fail range” is the key to long-term retention.

the substance, not the label,

There is no point in calling yourself “The Teal Organization.” Understanding the principles and adopting them in the context of your organization is the essence. Remember to follow the substance, not the form.

excerpt.rendered: We introduced the theory of the Teal organization, but the scene was confused and derailed — there are more and more such failures. Break down the gap between theory and practice realistically, touching on Frederick Laroux's original work.

title.rendered: 「ティール組織」を本当に機能させるには
Beginner Organizational Design: Rethinking Hierarchy

content.rendered:
As your business grows, it's time to rethink your hierarchical structure as you increasingly feel that your organization isn't moving and decision-making is slow. Rather than negating the hierarchy, organize the perspectives for redesign according to the purpose.

Typical pattern of hierarchies becoming dysfunctional

Hierarchy is inherently about making decisions more efficient. However, if you fall into the following pattern, it will have the opposite effect.

Pattern 1: Hierarchy acts as a barrier to information

The information that rises from the bottom up is filtered too much, and by the time it reaches the management, it is different from the actual situation. Conversely, information from above is also "translated" and transformed.

Pattern 2: Hierarchy creates accountability

"This is the upper judgment" and "This is the lower judgment" are pressed together, and nothing is decided.

Pattern 3: Hierarchy hinders personal growth

“Awaiting○○ manager approval” becomes a bottleneck and members cannot move voluntarily.

3 Perspectives on Redesigning Hierarchies

Perspective 1: Whether the hierarchy is for "decision" or "information transmission"

Many organizations combine both in one hierarchy, but the design that separates them is effective. The hierarchy for decision is shallow, and the hierarchy for information transmission is designed separately.

Perspective 2: Reconfiguring Span of Control

Consciously design “how many people to see per manager”. There are optimal values depending on the business characteristics, such as 5 to 7 people for creative work and 15 to 20 people for routine work.

Perspective 3: Separate Role and Authority

Dismantle "having a position = having the right to decide". Position is defined separately in the RACI for career path and authority is defined in “Who decides on this case”.

Redesign process

It's important to take steps. Organizational changes that abolish the hierarchy altogether will fail. It is safe to document decision-making authority first, then review the flow of information, and finally change the hierarchy itself.

Monitoring after redesign

3 months, 6 months and 1 year after the redesign. Continuously monitor changes in decision-making speed, member autonomy, and engagement, and make fine-tuning adjustments as needed.

excerpt.rendered: When you feel that your organization will stop moving as your business grows, the first thing you should do is rethink your hierarchy. Rather than denying the hierarchy, we will discuss practical perspectives for redesigning according to purpose and the specific redesign process.

4 indicators of psychological safety and their pitfalls

content.rendered:
The need to “measure” psychological safety is growing, but miscellaneous measurements can be detrimental to the organization. Edmondson's seven-item scale is well known, but if you use it as it is in Japanese companies, there is a tendency to have a large answer bias. This article explains a practical measurement approach.

Limitations of the survey alone

Surveys are useful, but they have intrinsic limitations. First, it depends on the subjectivity of the respondent. Second, the "indescribable atmosphere" itself is not reflected in the answer. Third, answer fatigue is prone to occur. To compensate for this, we need a combination of behavioral indicators.

4 Recommended Behavioral Indicators

Indicator 1: Frequency of failure reports

How often failures, mistakes, and issues are shared within the organization. The more psychologically safe an organization is, the sooner small failures are shared. Conversely, organizations with “zero reporting” are more likely to have a cover-up.

Indicator 2: Percentage of dissenting meetings

A meeting that ends in unanimity may not be a real agreement, but an agreement of silence. Monitor the percentage of meetings with healthy objections.

Metric 3: 1: 1 "troublesome" utterances

How well your manager listens to “what's bothering you” 1: 1. Although it is qualitative data, you can see the trend by reviewing it regularly.

Indicator 4: Degree of candor in interviews with retirees

How honest a retired person is. Psychological safety is a challenge for organizations that end up with a lot of things they couldn't say during their tenure.

4 pitfalls

Pitfall 1: Lower response rate due to survey fatigue

There are too many implementations every month. Quarterly is realistic.

Pitfall 2: Boss bias

Nomenclature does not bring out the real meaning. Guarantee of anonymity is essential.

Pitfall 3: Absolute Score Comparison

Instead of comparing to other companies or teams, you should look at how your team has changed over time.

Pitfall 4: Score leads directly to evaluation

As a direct result of the manager's evaluation, a score hack (induction of answers) will occur. We will adhere to the principle of treating it as data for improvement.

Measurement is a tool to see change

Rather than competing for absolute values, use it as a tool to analyze the amount of change and factors in your organization. This is the biggest key to making the measurement work.

excerpt.rendered: An increasing number of surveys attempt to gauge psychological safety, which can fatigue an organization. How to choose metrics and break down common pitfalls into four. We will explain in detail how to combine the biases specific to Japanese companies, their correction methods, and behavioral indicators.

Remote Era New Employee Onboarding Design

content.rendered:
Onboarding new hires will not work if you simply move the design of the in-person premise to a remote location. In addition to communicating information, the three non-verbal elements of sharing tacit knowledge, building relationships, and grasping organizational culture are missing if not intentionally designed in a remote environment. This article introduces the 90 day plan design framework.

90 day plan 3 axis design

Axis 1: Relationships

Set up 1: 1s with at least 10 people within 30 days of joining the company. Include managers, colleagues, adjacent departments, and management in a well-balanced manner. It is important to have a place for dialogue aimed at "knowing as a person" rather than "business necessities".

Axis 2: Performance

Align expectations at 30/60/90 day checkpoints. Day 30: Environment Setup Complete & Easy Contribution. Day 60: Complete one task independently. Day 90: Normal operation as part of a team. Document specific success criteria at each stage.

Axis 3: Culture

Set up a session to illustrate “decisions that are like our company.” Case studies reflecting on past project decision-making processes and opportunities for dialogue with founders and management.

Specific measures on each axis

Relationship Axis: Contemporary Design of the Buddy System

You can pair a sync joiner or officially assign a mentor. It is important to intentionally create a relationship where you can talk outside of work. 30 minutes of casual chat once a week for 3 months.

Axis of Work: Document First

Consciously document what was tacit knowledge and aim for a state where new employees can read and drive themselves. The rule that “what is not in the document is written by the newcomer” is effective.

Cultural Axis: The Decision Making Experience

Share important past decisions, including why you made them. This provides guidance when a newcomer is asked to make the same kind of decision.

Common failures

Typical points include overloading the first day of orientation, ending with a formal assignment of a mentor, and skipping the third month retrospective. Especially the last “3-month retrospective” is the most valuable time for both the new joiner and the organization.

Effect from a long-term perspective

A well-designed 90-day plan can make a big difference in retention and performance a year from now. The return on investment is several times the cost of recruiting.

excerpt.rendered: Remote premise onboarding does not work with “face-to-face alternatives”. Organize your first 90 days into a 3-axis redesign: Relationships, Work, and Culture, along with common failure patterns and implementation templates.

title.rendered: リモート時代の新入社員オンボーディング設計
Why Swedish Fika makes you more productive

content.rendered:
Fika is the time spent in Sweden with colleagues around coffee and baked goods once or twice a day. It's not just a break, it's rooted in the organization as a place for dialogue that goes beyond the hierarchy. In this article, we will organize the essence of it and how to implement it in the remote era.

Why Fika Benefits Productivity

From the research results and field observations, the reasons for productivity can be summarized into three points.

1. Information circulates in an out-of-business context

In formal meetings, there is a lot of “off-agreement information.” In places like Fika, they are shared naturally. Many of the project's risk signals are found in small talk.

2. Reduce the psychological distance between people with different titles

The very act of drinking coffee at the same table temporarily eliminates the difference in titles. The effect of making it easier to speak up in later work cannot be ignored.

3. The break itself contributes to productivity

There is a limit to the amount of time you can focus in a row. Multiple studies have shown that intentional breaks restore cognitive performance.

Fika Implementation in the Remote Era

Virtual Fika Operations

Set "Virtual Fika" twice a week for 30 minutes in a random combination. The topic can be discussed outside of work. Automate random matching with tools like Slack's Donut Bot to reduce operational load.

Recognize "Fika hours" as business hours

Pushing outside of working hours has the opposite effect. Positioning “this is part of the job” as a company is key to ensuring participation rates.

Pitfalls during implementation

The three points of “compulsory participation”, “having an agenda” and “boss-led” destroy the essence of Fika. Time without purpose is the source of value for those who feel comfortable gathering casually.

Quantitative effects

In the data observed for the implementing companies, the frequency of cross-functional consultations increased by 25-40% after 3-6 months, and the turnover rate tended to decrease by an average of 20% after 6 months. Although it is not an immediate measure, it has an extremely high ROI as a long-term investment.

excerpt.rendered: More than just a coffee break, Sweden's Fika is at the heart of organizational culture. Re-interpretation in the remote era and how to incorporate it into Japanese teams are introduced on a case-by-case basis. It also explains productivity data and its quantitative impact on the organization.

title.rendered: スウェーデン式「Fika」が生産性を高める理由
5 signs that one-on-one meetings are falling apart

content.rendered:
Even if the initial introduction of 1: 1 is functional, it will continue to fall apart in half a year to one year. If you think of debris as a "member problem", it will not improve. Often there is a design level issue behind it. In this article, we'll go over the five signs, their root causes, and the order in which they were rebuilt.

Don't miss these 5 signs

Signal 1: It is becoming a place to check progress

"What I did last week" and "What I will do this week" will be the end of the 30 minutes. This is essentially a team meeting and doesn't make sense to use 1: 1 time.

Indication 2: More than 10% cancellation rate

If a manager cancels 2 times in a row, the member recognizes that their time is a low priority. Restoring trust once lost can take 3-6 months.

Signal 3: The agenda always comes out from the boss's side

Originally, 1: 1 is a time for members. At a time when the agenda is manager-led, there is a discrepancy between the purpose and the operation.

Indication 4: “Nothing troubles me” is a routine response

It may not really have been there in the beginning, but if you continue with this answer three times in a row, it is clear that there is a problem with “how to listen” or “relationship”.

Signal 5: Meeting minutes are formal

It is a state where the purpose of the template filling is, and it is a record that has no meaning even if it is reviewed later.

Rebuild order

Rebuilding is about order. Getting it in the wrong order has the opposite effect.

Step 1: Re-agree on objectives

The first step is to “Re-Agreed 1: 1 Purpose” with all managers. From the point where management rewrites "1: 1 is what time is for".

Step 2: Lighter Formatting

Relax the formatting and switch to a member-driven agenda setting. Limit the template to “Minimum Items”.

Step 3: Cross-learning between managers

Provide a monthly space for managers to share 1: 1s with each other. It has the effect of leveling the skill variation.

Skeletal transformation is 90% preventable

It's hard to rebuild something that has fallen apart once and for all. Incorporating the "semi-annual review of operations" into the plan from the time of introduction is the most cost-effective preventive measure.

title.rendered: 1on1ミーティングが形骸化する5つの兆候
デンマーク企業に学ぶフラットな組織運営

content.rendered:
デンマークの企業文化を語る上で頻出するのが「フラットさ」というキーワードです。しかし、これは単に階層を撤廃するという話ではありません。むしろ、意思決定のプロセスを透明化することで、肩書きに依存しない議論を成立させる仕組みです。本稿では、その背後にある原則と、日本企業がどう適用できるかを整理します。

フラットさを支える3つの原則

原則1: 提案は誰でも出せるが、決定の責任は明確である

「みんなで決める」ではなく、「みんなで議論し、責任者が決める」が正確な表現です。責任者が明確だからこそ、提案者も安心して声を上げられます。

原則2: 意思決定の理由が必ず文書化される

「なぜそう決めたか」が後から検証可能な形で残ります。これにより、肩書きや声の大きさではなく、論理の妥当性で議論が進むようになります。

原則3: 役職ではなく、その案件の責任者として発言する

会議で発言するときは「自分の役職」ではなく「この案件における自分の立場」を明示します。CTOであっても、特定プロジェクトでは一エンジニアとして発言する場面があります。

日本企業への現実的な適用

全てを真似る必要はありません。まずは経営会議の議事録を全社公開することから始めるだけでも、意思決定の透明性は大きく改善します。次に、意思決定権限を文書化する（誰が何を決めるかのマトリクスを作る）。最後に、役職呼びの慣習を見直す（「さん」付け統一など）。

よくある失敗パターン

「フラット組織」を掲げて階層を撤廃した結果、暗黙のヒエラルキーが生まれて事態が悪化するケースが頻発しています。原因は「フラットさを目的化してしまった」こと。フラットさは手段であり、目的は「より良い意思決定を素早く行うこと」です。手段と目的を混同しないよう注意が必要です。

Timeline to fixation

文化として定着するには最低2年、できれば3年は必要です。短期的な成果を求めず、長期投資として取り組む姿勢が重要になります。

title.rendered: デンマーク企業に学ぶフラットな組織運営
心理的安全性は「優しさ」ではない、最新の研究が示すこと

content.rendered:
心理的安全性は、いまだに「優しさ」や「衝突を避けること」と混同されがちです。しかしAmy Edmondsonをはじめとする研究者が一貫して強調しているのは、心理的安全性は「率直さの土台」であり、むしろ高い基準と並立するときに最も効果を発揮するという点です。本稿ではよくある誤解を解きほぐし、実践に落とすための視点を整理します。

よくある3つの誤解

誤解1: 「全員が心地よく過ごせる職場」と同義。実際は「言いづらいことを言える土台」であり、対立そのものを避けることではありません。誤解2: 「基準を下げれば達成しやすい」。実際は基準が高くても、それを率直に議論できる環境こそが学習速度を生みます。誤解3: 「やる気のあるチームには自然と備わる」。実際は意図的に設計しないと劣化します。

2×2マトリクスで組織を分類する

Edmondsonの提唱する2軸モデルは、「心理的安全性の高低」×「基準の高低」で4象限を作ります。

領域1: 安心ゾーン（高安全・低基準）

互いに優しいが成果は出ない。心地よいが学習しないチーム。スタートアップが陥りやすい停滞期の典型。

領域2: 不安ゾーン（低安全・低基準）

Neither lethargic nor challenging. The state of many "dysfunctional departments".

領域3: 不安ハイパフォーマンス（低安全・高基準）

恐怖駆動で短期的には成果が出るが、燃え尽き・離職・不正の温床。多くの伝統的な日本企業の管理職層。

領域4: 学習ゾーン（高安全・高基準）

目指すべき到達点。率直な議論と高い達成意欲が両立する状態。

明日から始める3つの実践

会議で意見が割れたとき、収束を急がず「対立そのもの」を歓迎する姿勢を示す。マネージャーが先に弱さを開示する（「私はここを判断できていない」）。失敗の事後検証で「個人の責任追及」と「仕組みの改善」を明確に分ける。

計測と注意点

サーベイ計測は有効ですが、スコアの絶対値だけを追うと逆効果になります。「変化量」と「自由記述の質的変化」を併用するのが鉄則です。

title.rendered: 心理的安全性は「優しさ」ではない、最新の研究が示すこと
リモートワーク3年目で気づいた「沈黙の罠」

content.rendered:
リモートワークが定着して3年目を迎えるチームから、「会議で誰も発言しなくなった」「重要な懸念が事前にエスカレーションされない」という相談が増えています。これは個々のメンバーのモチベーション低下ではなく、構造的な問題として捉えるべきです。本稿では、沈黙が増える要因を3層に分解し、各層で打てる手を整理します。

沈黙が増える3つの構造的要因

第一に、リアクションコストの上昇です。対面なら頷きや表情で済む応答が、オンラインでは「ミュート解除→発言→ミュート」という明示的な行動を要求されます。1回あたり数秒の差ですが、心理的ハードルは想像以上に高い。第二に、雑談機会の消失です。給湯室・喫煙所・帰り道の数分が消えた結果、議題化されない「気がかり」を吐き出す場が無くなりました。第三に、評価の場と相談の場の分離が難しいこと。1on1が評価面談を兼ねていると、本音の相談が出にくくなります。

マネージャーが今日から取れる3つの打ち手

1. 会議冒頭の「全員一言ルーティン」

会議の最初の2分を「全員が一言話す」時間に固定します。話題は業務に限らず、週末の出来事でも構いません。発言の「最初の一歩」を集団で踏むことで、その後の議論への参加率が劇的に上がります。

2. 1on1で必ず「言いづらかったこと」を聞く

「最近、言おうか迷ったことはありますか？」をテンプレートとして毎回入れます。最初の数回は「特にない」で済まされても、3回目以降から本音が出始めるのが一般的です。

3. チャット上の「目に見えるリアクション」を増やす

テキストのみのコミュニケーションでは、応答の薄さがメンバーの「無視されている感」を生みます。マネージャーが意識的に絵文字リアクション・スレッド返信を増やすだけで、チーム全体の発信量が増えていきます。

組織として整えるべき構造

個人の努力に依存しないために、組織レベルでも仕組みを用意します。匿名の月次サーベイで「言えなかったこと」を集める、毎月1回「失敗事例の共有会」を持つ、心理的安全性スコアを KPI として可視化する。これらは個別のマネジメントスキルでは補えない領域です。

沈黙は症状ではなく信号

沈黙を「メンバーのやる気の問題」と片付けると、原因に手が届きません。沈黙はチームの構造に何かが起きていることを示す信号として、まず観察対象にする姿勢が出発点となります。

excerpt.rendered: リモート定着の3年目に多発する「言いたいことが言われなくなる」現象。心理的安全性が低下しているチームに共通するサインと、マネージャーが取るべき具体的アクション、そして組織として整えるべき構造的な打ち手まで整理します。

大学院では組織心理学を専攻し、修士号取得。研究と実務の両面から組織を見る視点を大切にしている。最近の関心領域は、リモート時代のチームビルディングと、評価制度の納得感設計。
